title: Intermediate Topics in Engineering Design (0.5 Unit)
description: Topics suggested by students and faculty and approved by the institute.

title: Designing Your Life (1 Unit)
description: Considers an approach to life as a series of design projects to help students craft a total life. Includes seminar-style discussions, role-playing, short writing assignments, hands-on making, guest speakers, and individual mentoring and coaching.

title: Human-Centered Service Design (1 Unit)
description: Project-based, human-centered design approach to service design, with a focus on the design of new or improved services that tap deeply into people's needs for connectedness, belonging, and autonomy. Project outcomes may include organizational structures, service designs, and designed products.

title: User Experience Design (1 Unit)
description: Project-based studio course covering the full range of user experience design, from screen-based experience to interaction with physical products to end-to-end environment design.

title: Human-Centered Product Design (1 Unit)
description: Project-based course focusing on user needs: observational methods, brainstorming, prototyping, business models, and the social and engineering concerns for product design.

title: Introduction to Industrial Design Methods (1 Unit)
description: Introduces the process of product development from an industrial design perspective, with a focus on exploring form through design sketching.

title: Advanced Solid Modeling (0.5 Unit)
description: Provides advanced instruction on the use of CAD modeling using Solidworks software.

title: Rendering (0.5 Unit)
description: Provides an introduction to Keyshot software, a photorealistic rendering package.

title: Performance and Technology: Composition Workshop (1 Unit)
description: In this course students will use basic mechatronics to create compelling movement-based performances. The course will involve workshop exploration of technologies embedded in performance: robots, media, computer interface. Students will create performance projects and discuss theoretical and historical implications of technologies in performance. Hands-on making and engineering workshops will be incorporated to develop skills in technological crafts such as circuit design and fabrication, toward technologically enhanced performance.

title: Computer-Aided Manufacturing (0.5 Unit)
description: Teaches the NX manufacturing environment to program machining operations for CNC milling, as well as the operation of CNC milling machines. Teaches the complete path from part design to manufacture, including operations, tools, and geometry in NX, manufacturing setup and g-code generation, proper machine setup, operation, troubleshooting and optimization for both 2.5D machining and full CNC machining.

title: Manufacturing Methods for Product Design (1 Unit)
description: An introduction to manufacturing processes including casting, injection molding, additive manufacturing, extrusion, machining, joining, and forming, using materials commonly found in modern consumer and industrial products. In weekly hands-on labs, students apply theory of manufacturing processes to the design of parts and process tooling for various fabrication methods. Focus will be placed on adjusting design and process parameters to obtain an optimal outcome for a given manufacturing process.

title: Rapid Prototyping (0.5-1 Unit)
description: A survey of additive manufacturing methods and hands-on training in the operation of all equipment in the Northwestern Rapid Prototyping lab. Students review the fundamentals and theory behind rapid prototyping methods, materials, applications of RP technology, advanced CAD modeling, and reverse engineering methods. Includes lab work, where students will learn the operation of additive manufacturing machines, laser scanners, and CAD surfacing software.

title: Bay Area Design Practicum (1 Unit)
description: For Bay Area Immersion Program Participants. This course is an opportunity to immerse in a critical area of need and make the most of our opportunity to engage with people in the Bay Area, joining them as visionaries, thought leaders and activists concerned with shaping our communities and world. This is a dynamic, hands-on studio course requiring project-based work and team collaboration. In this 300-level class, you are expected to be able to rapidly engage in the design process, working with multidisciplinary teams on uncovering opportunities, exploring ideas, prototyping solutions (whether physical, digital or interpersonal), and interacting with users.

title: Portfolio Development & Presentation (1 Unit)
description: Creation of an individual portfolio that showcase design work and further career goals. The portfolio physically presents a story that embodies its creator's goals.

title: Industrial Design Projects I (1 Unit)
description: Design thinking, user-centric principles of design and DFM. Development of an industrial design project for your personal portfolio. Concept ideation and sketching; use of discovery research and data visualization; problem framing and prototyping; design for manufacturing, Keyshot rendering, rapid prototyping. Pt 1 in two-course sequence; sequence must be taken in consecutive quarters.

title: Industrial Design Projects II (1 Unit)
description: Design thinking, user-centric principles of design and DFM. Development of an industrial design project for your personal portfolio. Concept ideation and sketching; use of discovery research and data visualization; problem framing and prototyping; design for manufacturing, Keyshot rendering, rapid prototyping.

title: Digital Product Development (2 Units)
description: "In this digital product development course, students will experience the full end-to-end product development lifecycle through ideation, build, and continuous improvement. Students will have an opportunity to explore and apply design thinking and agile/scrum methodologies to a client sponsored project. This class is by application only.

title: Service Design Studio I (1 Unit)
description: Explores the human centered approach to the design of services. Students will apply design thinking to client sponsored projects and synthesize both user and client needs to the design of tangible consumer touch points. This includes experience plans, digital interface designs, communication models, organizational designs, systems and/or brand tonality deliverables. Pt 1 in two-course sequence; sequence must be taken in consecutive quarters.

title: Service Design Studio II (1 Unit)
description: Explores the human centered approach to the design of services. Students will apply design thinking to client sponsored projects and synthesize both user and client needs to the design of tangible consumer touch points. This includes experience plans, digital interface designs, communication models, organizational designs, systems and/or brand tonality deliverables.

title: Interdisciplinary Product Design Projects I (1 Unit)
description: Open-ended, team-based product or system design projects in real-world settings. Project research, concept development, professional communication, advanced topics in design. One of DSGN 305-0 , DSGN 306-0 , or DSGN 308-0 is recommended before taking this course. Pt 1 in two-course sequence; sequence must be taken in consecutive quarters.

title: Interdisciplinary Product Design Projects II (1 Unit)
description: Open-ended, team-based product or system design projects in real-world settings. Implementation, evaluation, communication, documentation.

title: Manufacturing Engineering Design (1 Unit)
description: A hands-on, team-based survey of lean manufacturing concepts such as DFA (design for assembly), DFM (design for manufacturing), automation, quality control, process planning, tooling design, concurrent engineering, and continuous improvement. Students are given the components of an existing product and are challenged to design the manufacturing specifications and process. Design strategy, manufacturing modeling and optimization, engineering documentation, quality control, manufacturing costing and product manufacturing productivity skills are put into practice in a final pilot run of the manufacturing process by each team.

title: MaDE Capstone Sequence I (1 Unit)
description: The first quarter of a three-quarter, year-long product design and development experience for students in the MaDE program. Senior standing and instructor permission are required.

title: MaDE Capstone Sequence II (1 Unit)
description: The second quarterr of a three-quarter, year-long product design and development experience for students in the MaDE program. Senior standing and instructor permission are required.

title: MaDE Capstone Sequence III (1 Unit)
description: The third and final quarter of a three-quarter, year-long product design and development experience for students in the MaDE program. Senior standing and instructor permission are required.

title: Advanced Topics in Design (0.5 Unit)
description: Topics suggested by students and faculty and approved by the institute.

title: Independent Study Project (1 Unit)
description: Independent study on a design topic supervised by a faculty member.

title: Earth Science for the 21st Century (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to earth science through topical issues facing contemporary society. Evolution of the earth, geologic hazards, natural resources, peak oil, climate change, the water cycle, nuclear fuel cycle, geology of US national parks.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Climate Catastrophes in Earth History (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to fundamental components of the earth system that control climate. Exploration of present-day climate change and how climate has changed (sometimes catastrophically) in the geologic past.

title: The Ocean, the Atmosphere & Our Climate (1 Unit)
description: The role of the world's oceans in the earth's climate system. Properties of the oceans and marine life. Interaction of oceans, atmosphere, and land.

title: Evolution and the Scientific Method (1 Unit)
description: Review of evolutionary theory and its scientific, philosophical, social, and religious impacts.

title: Fantasy Worlds – How to Build Your Own Planet (1 Unit)
description: The formation and evolution of rocky planets. Introduction of physical concepts common in the lives of planets as they are in our everyday lives: gravity, heat transport, magnetism, and others. Students will apply these concepts to build their own unique planet, and will present their creation at a culminating poster presentation.

title: Earth System History (1 Unit)
description: Evolution of the earth system and its record through geological time. Interactions among the atmosphere, hydrosphere, sediments, and life on earth. Recommended Background: At least one credit in math, chemistry, biology or physics.

title: Earth Systems Science and Climate Change (1 Unit)
description: This course covers Earth systems: atmosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and geosphere, and their interactions at local and global scales. Topics include an introduction to systems thinking, how Earth processes formed and modify surface environments and climate, the planetary history of Earth systems, and modern human impacts on Earth and climate. Students may not enroll if they have previously completed EARTH 203-0 or ENVR_SCI 201-0.

title: Data Analysis in Earth and Environmental Sciences (1 Unit)
description: Overview of data analysis and modeling approaches used in earth, environmental, and planetary sciences, including data standards, environmental statistics, box modeling, geospatial and geo-temporal analyses. Assessments focus on skill-mastery, and include small group mini-projects. The data sets used span sub-disciplines of earth and environmental sciences, such as population, climate, solid earth, and water science.

title: Ecology & Environmental Change (1 Unit)
description: Dimensions of the ecological niche; growth and regulation of populations; interactions among populations; community structure and diversity; conservation. Students may not enroll if they have previously completed ENVR_SCI 202.

title: Decision-Making in the Anthropocene (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to human interactions with the environment. Topics may include but are not limited to energy, sustainability, pollution, and climate change. Students may not enroll if they have previously completed ENVR SCI 203.

title: Physical Earth Science (1 Unit)
description: Physical Earth Science synthesizes physical geology and historical geology. Physical geology examines the creation, modification, and characterization of rocks and minerals, as well as internal and external processes that shape the planet. Historical geology examines the origin and evolution of Earth and life through time. The course includes required laboratory sections and culminates with a required weekend field trip.

title: Physics of the Earth's Interior (1 Unit)
description: The earth as a planet: origin, composition, and evolution of the solar system and the earth; internal structure of the earth; plate tectonics. Recommended Background: At least one credit in math, chemistry, biology or physics.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Special problems under direct faculty supervision. Comprehensive report required. Consent of instructor required.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Introduction to Macroeconomics (1 Unit)
description: An introduction to economics with emphasis on macroeconomics. Topics include: scarcity and choice, elements of supply and demand, inflation, unemployment, recessions, booms, fiscal and monetary policy, international balance of payments, and budget deficits.

title: Introduction to Microeconomics (1 Unit)
description: An introductory course on the fundamentals of microeconomics.  The behavior of individuals and firms in deciding on prices and allocation of scarce resources.  Topics include: consumer preferences, costs of production, equilibrium prices and output, different market types, potential market failures, and the role of government interventions and public policy.

title: Business Strategy (1 Unit)
description: Firms' choices of prices, capacity, location, quality, variety, investment and product innovation when navigating complex economic environments shaped by government policy and inter-firm rivalries. (Majors and Minors should not take this course, but should take ECON 349-0 instead. Students may not receive credit if they have completed ECON 349-0 ).

title: Introduction to Applied Econometrics (1 Unit)
description: An introduction to econometrics. The underlying theory of regression and the practical application of these techniques to data sets. Understanding and diagnosing common statistical problems encountered during estimation. All other substitutions (including AP Statistics) must be cleared through the Director of Undergraduate Studies for Economics.

title: Senior Seminar (1 Unit)
description: For students of superior ability. Original research on a topic of interest to the student, culminating in a senior thesis. By department invitation only. Grade of K given in 398-1.

title: Senior Seminar (1 Unit)
description: For students of superior ability. Original research on a topic of interest to the student, culminating in a senior thesis. By department invitation only.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Advanced work through reading, research, and discussion to build on economics coursework taken by the student. Project to be decided by mutual agreement with a faculty member.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Expository Writing (1 Unit)
description: Emphasizes all phases of the composition process, research methods, and critical thinking. Careful review of student papers and reports. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Writing in Special Contexts (0.5 Unit)
description: Introduction to expository writing similar to ENGLISH 105-0 but paired with a course in another discipline.

title: Writing in Special Contexts (0.5 Unit)
description: Introduction to expository writing similar to ENGLISH 105-0 but paired with a course in another discipline.

title: Literary Histories (1 Unit)
description: Content varies, but all versions of this course engage with at least 200 years of literature from a particular tradition, genre, or theme, with an emphasis on literary history and narratives of continuity and change over time. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: Introduction to Creative Writing (1 Unit)
description: Forms and techniques of poetry, fiction, and creative nonfiction.

title: Intermediate Composition (1 Unit)
description: Expository writing at an intermediate level. Emphasis on techniques for writing clearly, precisely, and persuasively. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Reading & Writing Poetry (1 Unit)
description: Forms and techniques of verse. May not be taken earlier than winter quarter of the first year. Seniors may enroll only with department consent.

title: Reading and Writing Fiction (1 Unit)
description: Forms and techniques of fiction.

title: Reading & Writing Creative Non-Fiction (1 Unit)
description: Forms and techniques of creative nonfiction.

title: Topics in Genre Writing (1 Unit)
description: Forms and techniques of genre writing (e.g., screenwriting, young adult fiction, adaptation, memoir). May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: British Literary Traditions (1 Unit)
description: Chronological survey of British literature in its cultural contexts from Beowulf to the 18th century. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: British Literary Traditions (1 Unit)
description: Chronological survey of British literature in its cultural contexts from the late 18th century to the present day. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Introduction to Poetry (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to poetic analysis across a wide range of authors and periods, with emphasis on poetry’s formal elements, genre conventions, and the historical, material, and cultural conditions that shape them. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Introduction to Drama (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to the fundamental elements of drama as perceived in performance, exploring how dramatic works communicate from text to stage to audience. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Introduction to Fiction (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to the analysis of prose fiction from the 18th century to the present day, with special attention to narrative strategies and their historical and cultural contexts. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Introduction to Film and Its Literatures (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to the theory and practice of formal film analysis. Students will gain a critical overview of multiple forms of film-related writing, including historical scholarship, film theory, popular reviews, legal documents, manifestos, and movie-inspired fiction. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Topics in Literature, Film, and Media (1 Unit)
description: In contrast to ENGLISH 214-0 , which introduces students to a wider mix of literary, cinematic, and media traditions, this course explores specific topics, forms, periods, or genres in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: The Bible as Literature (1 Unit)
description: Selected books of the Hebrew bible and New Testament studied from a literary perspective. Students will consider issues of plot, character, genre, narrative strategy, and theories of interpretation. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. Students may not receive credit if they took the same class as the former COMP_LIT 210-0.

title: Introduction to Shakespeare (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to representative Shakespearean plays, including comedies, tragedies, and romances. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Introduction to Postcolonial Literature (1 Unit)
description: Introduces the field of postcolonial literary studies, including a critical overview of key terms and major debates. Students will read selected postcolonial literary works, paying particular attention to their formal properties, alongside theoretical and historical texts on colonialism and its aftermath. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Introduction to African American Literature (1 Unit)
description: Literature of Black people in the United States from slavery to freedom. Works of major writers and significant but unsung bards of the past. ENGLISH 266-0 and BLK_ST 210-0 are taught together; may receive credit for only 1 of these courses. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Topics in African American Literature (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. In contrast to ENGLISH 266-0 , which offers a broad survey of the field, this course explores particular topics in African American literature in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: American Literary Traditions (1 Unit)
description: Representative writers and works of American literature in cultural context. Works discussed range from culture contact to the Civil War. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: American Literary Traditions (1 Unit)
description: Representative 19th- and 20th-century works of American literature in cultural context. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Introduction to 20th-Century American Literature (1 Unit)
description: Representative works of American literature since World War I. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Introduction to Native American and Indigenous Literatures (1 Unit)
description: Survey of Native American and Indigenous literatures from pre-contact periods to the present. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected.

title: Introduction to Asian American Literature (1 Unit)
description: Survey of Asian American literature from the early 20th century to the present, covering a range of genres and ethnicities. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. Taught with ASIAN_AM 275-0 ; may receive credit for only 1 of these courses.

title: Topics in Asian American Literature (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. In contrast to ENGLISH 275-0 , which offers a broad survey of the field, this course explores particular topics in Asian American literature in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. Taught with ASIAN_AM 276-0 . May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: Introduction to Latinx Literature (1 Unit)
description: Surveys major writers and movements from the Spanish colonial era to the present, covering a range of genres and ethnicities. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. Taught with LATINO 277-0 and SPANISH 277-0 ; may receive credit for only 1 of these courses.

title: Topics in Multiethnic Literature (1 Unit)
description: Although content varies depending on the specific course topic, all versions of this course examine the ways in which American ethnic identity has been constructed and discussed through literature. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: Topics in Postcolonial & Comparative Literatures (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. In contrast to ENGLISH 280-0 , which offers a broad survey of the field, this course explores particular topics in postcolonial and comparative literatures in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: Writing and Speaking in Business (1 Unit)
description: Emphasizes writing and speaking to inform and persuade audiences in business contexts to achieve business goals. Attention to clear, compelling, and well-organized written and oral communication.

title: Introduction to Literature and the Environment (1 Unit)
description: Studies in literature and other media oriented by ecological thinking. An introduction to the ways in which language, literature, and aesthetic production shape ideas about nature, varying widely across historical and cultural contexts. Suitable for majors and nonmajors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. ENGLISH 283-0 taught with ENVR_POL 283-0 ; may not receive credit for both.

title: Topics in Literature and the Environment (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. In contrast to ENGLISH 283-0 , which offers a broad survey of the field, this course explores particular topics in greater depth. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic. ENGLISH 284-0 and ENVR_POL 284-0 are taught together.

title: Topics in Literature and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore literary texts in their cultural contexts. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: Topics in Global Literatures (1 Unit)
description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore literary texts written outside of Britain and the United States. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: Topics in Literature and Ethics (1 Unit)
description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore literary texts that emerge from, and speak back to, ethical and/or religious traditions. Suitable for majors and non-majors. No prior knowledge of the field is expected. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: Seminar in Reading and Interpretation (1 Unit)
description: Close reading of literary works in the light of various critical methodologies in literary study, with an emphasis on developing skills in qualitative analysis and oral and written expression. Required for English literature majors and minors and Creative Writing majors; recommended for Creative Writing minors. Offers good preparation for higher-level literary study. May be taken only once.

title: Practical Rhetoric (1 Unit)
description: The theory of writing and skills that underlie good writing; primarily for teachers in secondary schools and universities.

title: Advanced Composition (1 Unit)
description: This course is for undergraduate students in their second year or above who want to develop their abilities to write in different styles and for different audiences.

title: Advanced Poetry Writing (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Advanced Fiction Writing (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Advanced Creative Nonfiction Writing (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Advanced Creative Cross-Genre Writing (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated twice for credit with a different topic.

title: Studies in Asian American Literature (1 Unit)
description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore Asian American literature in its cultural contexts. Selected texts range from the early 20th century to the present and engage with a range of genres and ethnicities. Recommended for students with prior coursework in English, Asian American Studies, or a related discipline. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: Topics in Latinx Literature (1 Unit)
description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course examine Latinx literature in its cultural contexts. Selected texts range from the Spanish colonial era to the present and engage with a range of genres and ethnicities. Recommended for students with prior coursework in English, Latinx Studies, or a related discipline. May be repeated for credit with a different topic. ENGLISH 377-0 taught with LATINO 377-0 .

title: Literature & Medicine (1 Unit)
description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore medicine through a variety of literary media. Potential subjects of study include illness, ageing, medical treatment, wellness culture, and doctor-patient relationships. Recommended for students with prior coursework in English, pre-health fields, or related disciplines. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: Literature and Law (1 Unit)
description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore the representation of law in literature and culture, and how law uses language. Students may perform literary analyses of legal discourse alongside literary texts. Recommended for students with prior coursework in English, Legal Studies, or a related discipline. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Studies in Literature and Ethics (1 Unit)
description: Although content varies depending on the course topic, all versions of this course explore literary texts that emerge from, and speak back to, ethical and/or religious traditions. Recommended for students with prior coursework in English, Religious Studies, Philosophy, or related disciplines. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: The Situation of Writing (1 Unit)
description: The sociology of writers, writing, publication, dissemination of literature, and reading.

title: Theory and Practice of Poetry (1 Unit)
description: Tenets of poetry in English, including prosody, form, metaphor, voice, experimentation; involves intensive writing practice and culminates in the production of a long poem.

title: Theory and Practice of Poetry (1 Unit)
description: Tenets of poetry in English, including prosody, form, metaphor, voice, experimentation; involves intensive writing practice and culminates in the production of a long poem.

title: Theory and Practice of Poetry (1 Unit)
description: Tenets of poetry in English, including prosody, form, metaphor, voice, experimentation; involves intensive writing practice and culminates in the production of a long poem.

title: Theory & Practice of Fiction (1 Unit)
description: Tenets of fictional realism and its substitutes; practice in different applications of plot, narrative technique, point of view; culminates in the writing of a novella.

title: Theory & Practice of Fiction (1 Unit)
description: Tenets of fictional realism and its substitutes; practice in different applications of plot, narrative technique, point of view; culminates in the writing of a novella.

title: Theory & Practice of Fiction (1 Unit)
description: Tenets of fictional realism and its substitutes; practice in different applications of plot, narrative technique, point of view; culminates in the writing of a novella.

title: Theory and Practice of Creative Nonfiction (1 Unit)
description: Tenets of creative nonfiction; practice in different styles, form, and modes; culminates in the writing of a long creative nonfiction project.

title: Theory and Practice of Creative Nonfiction (1 Unit)
description: Tenets of creative nonfiction; practice in different styles, form, and modes; culminates in the writing of a long creative nonfiction project.

title: Theory and Practice of Creative Nonfiction (1 Unit)
description: Tenets of creative nonfiction; practice in different styles, form, and modes; culminates in the writing of a long creative nonfiction project.

title: Honors Sequence (1 Unit)
description: The first course in a two-course sequence for seniors preparing an honors essay in the literature major. Students pursue individual research topics under the direction of the departmental honors coordinator and a faculty advisor. Admission by application only. Students will receive a K grade pending completion of the honors thesis.

title: Honors Sequence (1 Unit)
description: The second course in a two-course sequence for seniors preparing an honors essay in the literature major. Students pursue individual research topics under the direction of the departmental honors coordinator and a faculty advisor. Admission by application only. Students will receive a K grade pending completion of the honors thesis.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Individual projects with faculty guidance. Open to majors with junior or senior standing and to senior minors. May be elected two times, but only 1 unit at a time.

title: Principles of Entrepreneurship (1 Unit)
description: This course serves as the foundational course for the undergraduate minor in entrepreneurship. This survey-style course is a good choice for students who wish to familiarize themselves with the world of entrepreneurship. Students will learn to think like entrepreneurs and will be introduced to key topics relevant to entrepreneurship, including customer discovery, finance, and marketing. They will also learn how to understand and perform analyses of a broad range of entrepreneurial activities related to different applications of entrepreneurship. Taught with IEMS 225-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Financing Entrepreneurial Ventures (1 Unit)
description: Topics selected from work of current interest in entrepreneurship and innovation. May be repeated for credit.

title: Leadership, Ethics, and You (1 Unit)
description: This class satisfies a core requirement for the undergraduate minor in entrepreneurship. The class is designed for students who want to explore how ethics and integrity are tied to leadership, and how leadership skills can contribute to future success in both entrepreneurial endeavors and traditional career paths.

title: Tech Ethics and Business Integrity (1 Unit)
description: This course will explore the unique ethical challenges that face the technology industry, utilizing recent and historical case studies. Students will be challenged to think about what it means to run a business with integrity by examining common issues within the workplace as well as the responsibility that businesses have to customers. Data privacy abuses, sexual misconduct, and corporate greed, among many other topics, will be addressed.

title: Special Topics (1 Unit)
description: Topics selected from work of current interest in entrepreneurship and innovation. May be repeated for credit.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Elementary French (1 Unit)
description: The first course of a three-quarter sequence for true beginners. This course covers grammar, vocabulary, reading, writing, conversation and culture. The aim of the course is to learn and develop skills in speaking, understanding, reading, writing and cultural competence. Class will be conducted exclusively in French.

title: Elementary French (1 Unit)
description: The second course of a three-quarter sequence for true beginners. This course covers grammar, vocabulary, reading, writing, conversation and culture. The aim of the course is to learn and develop skills in speaking, understanding, reading, writing and cultural competence. Class will be conducted exclusively in French.

title: Elementary French (1 Unit)
description: The third course of a three-quarter sequence for true beginners. This course covers grammar, vocabulary, reading, writing, conversation and culture. The aim of the course is to learn and develop skills in speaking, understanding, reading, writing and cultural competence. Class will be conducted exclusively in French.

title: Intensive Elementary French (1 Unit)
description: The first course of a two-quarter sequence. This accelerated first-year French course covers the same material as the three-quarter sequence of French 111, but which assumes some prior knowledge of the language. The aim of the course is to review and develop skills in speaking, understanding, reading and writing French through study, practice, and class activities.

title: Intensive Elementary French (1 Unit)
description: The second course of a two-quarter sequence. This accelerated first-year French course covers the same material as the three-quarter sequence of French 111, but which assumes some prior knowledge of the language. The aim of the course is to review and develop skills in speaking, understanding, reading and writing French through study, practice, and class activities.

title: Intermediate French (1 Unit)
description: First course in a three-quarter sequence in Intermediate French. Further development in French language and culture, emphasizing grammar review, listening comprehension, conversation, reading, and writing.

title: Intermediate French (1 Unit)
description: Second course in a three-quarter sequence in Intermediate French. Further development in French language and culture, emphasizing grammar review, listening comprehension, conversation, reading, and writing.

title: Intermediate French (1 Unit)
description: Third course in a three-quarter sequence in Intermediate French. Further development in French language and culture, emphasizing grammar review, listening comprehension, conversation, reading, and writing.

title: Intensive Intermediate French (1 Unit)
description: French language and culture: conversation, composition, reading of cultural and literary texts, and grammar review. Three class meetings a week.

title: Intensive Intermediate French (1 Unit)
description: French language and culture: conversation, composition, reading of cultural and literary texts, and grammar review. Three class meetings a week.

title: Intensive Intermediate French (1 Unit)
description: French language and culture: conversation, composition, reading of cultural and literary texts, and grammar review. Three class meetings a week.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Credit for 1 quarter only.

title: Culture and Society (1 Unit)
description: Development of fluency, accuracy, and creativity in speaking, comprehension, reading, and writing French; introduction to social, cultural, and literary topics.

title: Writing Workshop: Cultural Encounters in Contemporary France (1 Unit)
description: Practical study of French grammar and structure; students develop and improve writing skills through practice in preparing short compositions.

title: Oral Workshop: Individual and Society in France Today (1 Unit)
description: Practical course to increase listening comprehension, build vocabulary and idiom use, and enhance communication skills.

title: Acting French (1 Unit)
description: Use of dramatic scenes, dialogues, songs and performance to help students improve their language skills and develop their interpretive, interpersonal and intercultural competence at the Intermediate Mid/High level.

title: Reading Literatures in French (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to texts in various genres such as essay, poetry, drama, novel, and autobiography, from at least two periods from the Middle Ages to the present.

title: Reading Cultures in French (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to French and/or francophone cultures through texts and media from at least two periods; major themes, issues, and debates.

title: Introducing the Novel (1 Unit)
description: Textual interpretation and analysis of French novels from different periods, with special attention to formal issues.

title: Introducing Theatre (1 Unit)
description: Textual interpretation and analysis of French plays from different periods, with special attention to formal issues. Principles of tragedy and comedy; contemporary developments.

title: Introducing Poetry (1 Unit)
description: Textual interpretation and analysis of French poetry from different periods, with special attention to formal issues. Overview of major poetic movements.

title: French Existentialism (1 Unit)
description: Existentialism in its literary, philosophical, and cultural manifestations.

title: Theater in Translation (1 Unit)
description: Representative French plays from the 17th through 20th centuries; basic concepts of genre; social and historical context. Credit not allowed for both FRENCH 279-0 and FRENCH 272-0 .

title: Major Topics in French and Francophone Studies (1 Unit)
description: An introduction to major themes or problems in the study of French and Francophone studies for a wide audience. Potential topics include: literary approaches to scientific language, representing the 19th century in film, the French novel in translation, laughing in French, etc. Taught in English.

title: French Phonetics (1 Unit)
description: Development of near-native spoken French through practice in correct pronunciation. Phonetic system of contemporary French; introduction to basic issues of theoretical phonetics.

title: Advanced Language in Context: Society and Popular Culture (1 Unit)
description: Practical study of structure, syntax, and usage of French through contemporary media, cinema, theater, and popular culture.

title: Advanced Writing: Finding Your Voice in French (1 Unit)
description: Development of written expression for different communicative needs and functions based on the study of French writing styles and techniques.

title: Advanced Conversation: Debating Contemporary France (1 Unit)
description: Development of advanced proficiency and confidence in spoken French through practice of speech and discussion of issues in current French media and culture. Emphasis on culturally appropriate usage.

title: French For Professions (1 Unit)
description: French language as used in professional contexts. May include study of a specific field and differences from its American counterpart. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: The Middle Ages & Renaissance (1 Unit)
description: Study of literary texts of the French Middle Ages and Renaissance with emphasis on their historical and literary-historical contexts.

title: Medieval French Narratives (1 Unit)
description: Major narrative works of the French Middle Ages in historical context. Content varies; may include epics such as the Song of Roland, romances such as Chrétien de Troyes's Perceval, and narratives of childhood. Texts read in modern French versions.

title: Topics in Renaissance Literature (1 Unit)
description: Study of literary and other texts of the French Renaissance with emphasis on their literary, historical, and political contexts. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Montaigne and Modernity (1 Unit)
description: In-depth study of the work of Michel de Montaigne and his models within the context of Renaissance history, politics and philosophy. All readings and discussion in French.

title: 17th Century Literature (1 Unit)
description: Topics and issues related to the literature and culture of 17th century France. Content varies; topics covered previously include theater and its social and political contexts, the rise of rational thought, and the development of fiction and poetry. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Sexual Politics and the Ancien Regime (1 Unit)
description: Literary, intellectual, and political role of women in view of the debates generated by the issues of women's power in the public sphere before the French Revolution.

title: Rousseau and the French Revolution (1 Unit)
description: Analysis of Rousseau's political thought and major literary works and their impact on Revolutionary ideology and culture.

title: Studies in the Enlightenment (1 Unit)
description: Authors such as Rousseau, Diderot, Montesquieu, Voltaire, and Graffigny in relation to Enlightenment debates about science, religion, political authority, human nature, colonialism, gender, and slavery.

title: The Novel in French (1 Unit)
description: Content varies; may include the novel of the ancien régime, the psychological novel, and the Bildungsroman in France.

title: Topics in Modern and Contemporary French Literature and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Transhistorical study of literary and other texts of the modern and contemporary periods (1800–present) with emphasis on their literary, historical, and political contexts. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: From Modernism to Postmodernism (1 Unit)
description: Crises and reinventions of French prose from the modernist moment of the early 20th century to the ambiguities of "engaged" literature of the 1930s to postmodernism.

title: African Literatures and Cultures (1 Unit)
description: Major issues, trends, and authors from francophone Africa. Content varies; may include Shahrazade, narratives of gender relations, law and literature, violence, and writing.

title: Caribbean Literatures and Cultures (1 Unit)
description: Major issues, trends, and authors from the francophone Caribbean and its diasporas. Content varies; may include Caribbean women writers; slavery, history, and memory; Caribbean identities.

title: The Maghreb and the Middle East (1 Unit)
description: Major issues in the literatures and cultures of North Africa and the Middle East. Content varies. May include exile in writing; politics of language and translation.

title: France and East Asia (1 Unit)
description: Interdisciplinary approaches to the history of French-East Asian relations, including French representations of East Asia. May include translation, japonisme, cinema, literary and philosophical avant-gardes, and culture and globalization.

title: Transnational Francophone Studies (1 Unit)
description: Exploration of cultural production in various genres from the French-speaking world, with an emphasis on themes, ideas, and/or forms that traverse national and/or cultural boundaries.

title: Giants, Cannibals, and Critique (1 Unit)
description: Analysis of works of Rabelais and Montaigne and their techniques of satire and social critique. Readings include related selections from Erasmus, More, La Boétie, and others.

title: Proust (1 Unit)
description: Introduces the works of Marcel Proust, a central figure of European literature and thought.

title: French Film (1 Unit)
description: Topics in French cinema: for example, French classical cinema, the New Wave, postcolonial French film, the cinema of Marguerite Duras.

title: Gender & Sexuality (1 Unit)
description: Major trends and perspectives in gender and sexuality studies such as first and second wave feminisms, lesbian writers, AIDS literature, queer theory, gender and orientalism, cross-cultural feminism.

title: Contemporary Theory (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to some major trends in contemporary French theory and the way they have influenced literary studies in the United States.

title: Topics in French Literature and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Advanced exploration of special topics in French studies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Political & Social Thought in France (1 Unit)
description: Major political and social trends in France from the ancien régime to the 20th century. Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Women Writing in French (1 Unit)
description: Analysis of texts by women authors with regard to their respective social, cultural, political, and historical contexts.

title: Gender & Writing (1 Unit)
description: Issues of gender and sexuality in the production of literary and other creative texts in various historical periods. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Topics in Literature and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Topics, issues, and questions in French and francophone culture. Content varies; may include French and francophone cinema, the intellectual in France. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Theory and Practice of Translation (1 Unit)
description: Intercultural communication through analysis of translation theories and translated works; translation exercises. Content varies; genres may include prose, poetry, graphic novels, and theater.

title: Foreign Language Teaching: Theory and Practice (1 Unit)
description: Theoretical foundation and practical applications of second-language acquisition and applied linguistics. Analysis and design of pedagogical materials. Self-reflection and analysis of teaching style and teaching philosophy.

title: Advanced Studies in Culture and Thought (1 Unit)
description: Theoretical perspectives and paradigms for understanding culture through in-depth study of a historical, cultural, or theoretical issue or of a literary or artistic work. Independent term paper.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Independent reading and research. Topics arranged through consultation with an instructor and approval of the department.

title: Beginning German (1 Unit)
description: This sequence offers students a systematic introduction to German language and culture emphasizing reading, writing, speaking, and listening comprehension.

title: Beginning German (1 Unit)
description: This sequence offers students a systematic introduction to German language and culture emphasizing reading, writing, speaking, and listening comprehension.

title: Beginning German (1 Unit)
description: This sequence offers students a systematic introduction to German language and culture emphasizing reading, writing, speaking, and listening comprehension.

title: Intermediate German (1 Unit)
description: This sequence offers students a systematic review of German language and culture. The class fosters learning in the four modalities: speaking, listening comprehension, reading, and writing.

title: Intermediate German (1 Unit)
description: This sequence offers students a systematic review of German language and culture. The class fosters learning in the four modalities: speaking, listening comprehension, reading, and writing.

title: Intermediate German (1 Unit)
description: This sequence offers students a systematic review of German language and culture. The class fosters learning in the four modalities: speaking, listening comprehension, reading, and writing.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Beginning German through Musical Journeys in Vienna (1 Unit)
description: Interdisciplinary course offering musically interested students the opportunity to acquire German language skills through an immersion in the musical and cultural history of Vienna.

title: Focus Reading (1 Unit)
description: Course for students who would like to explore German texts in more depth. Examines contemporary German culture. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.  (This course will not count for the language requirement but may be taken concurrently with GERMAN 102-3 ).

title: Focus Speaking (1 Unit)
description: Practical training in listening comprehension and speaking. Examines contemporary German culture. May be repeated for credit with change of topic. (This course will not count for the language requirement but may be taken concurrently with GERMAN 102-3 ).

title: Focus Writing (1 Unit)
description: Development of written proficiency in German through analysis and production of portraits, descriptions, narratives, reviews of films, reports, argumentative essays, advertisements, and interpretations of literary works.

title: Current Events in German Media (1 Unit)
description: Exploration of current events in a variety of German media (newspapers, TV, Internet, etc.). Topics include politics, music, film, sports, and cultural issues.

title: German in the Business World (1 Unit)
description: German language study oriented toward business-related communication situations, such as social interactions with customers, business travel, basic business letters.

title: German Culture through Film (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to 20th century German cinema. Discussion of German identity, culture, history, and politics. Course emphasizes cultural knowledge and German language skills.

title: History, Politics, and Culture in 21st Century German (1 Unit)
description: In-depth cultural and linguistic exploration of history, politics, and current issues (e.g., integration of foreigners, multicultural life) in Germany.

title: Introduction to German Literature: 1800-1900 (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to representative texts and writers of 19th century German literature. Familiarization with literary analysis and genres.

title: Introduction to German Literature: 1900-1945 (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to representative German texts and writers of the first half of the 20th century, when the First World War, the Weimar Republic, and the Third Reich marked the demise of the German Empire.

title: Introduction to German Literature: 1945-today (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to representative short stories by major German-speaking authors since 1945. The stories represent a dynamic period in German literature and highlight important social, political, and intellectual issues.

title: Contemporary Germany (1 Unit)
description: The German political, social, and cultural scene after 1945.

title: History of German Film (1 Unit)
description: In-depth study of German films and cultural background. Topics may vary-for example, the pioneer film or "new" German cinema.

title: Berlin and the Culture of Democracy (1 Unit)
description: History and culture of the city from 1900 to the present, including the Weimar period, Nazi regime, the divisions of the Cold War, and the newly unified capital.

title: The Theme of Faust Through the Ages (1 Unit)
description: Faust theme in literature and music through shifting intellectual and social climates from the 16th century to the present.

title: Jews and Germans: An Intercultural History I (1 Unit)
description: Exploration of Jewish encounters with German culture. German Jewry from the 18th century to the end of the 19th century, when Jews were granted legal standing as German citizens.

title: Jews and Germans: An Intercultural History II (1 Unit)
description: Jewish culture-German culture exploration. German-speaking Jewry from the late 19th century to 1933.

title: Kafka and Nietzsche (1 Unit)
description: Exploration of two key figures in German modernity. Analysis of the relation between philosophy and literature; inquiry into the idea of the "ascetic ideal."

title: Decadence and Desire: Turn-of-the-Century Vienna (1 Unit)
description: Literature and thought of fin de siècle Vienna and their impact on modern consciousness. Fiction, poetry, essays, and plays by Freud, Schnitzler, Wittgenstein, Hofmannsthal, Musil, Karl Kraus, and Schoenberg.

title: Imagining Modern Jewish Culture in Yiddish and German (1 Unit)
description: History and character of Yiddish and the development of modern German culture and German-Jewish culture. Appreciation of the variety of "Judaisms" imagined and reimagined during modern European history.

title: Analyzing Freud (1 Unit)
description: Freud's work from a comparative and interdisciplinary perspective. Fundamental texts by Freud in dialogue with related materials that situate him in historical, cultural, and intellectual context.

title: Special Topics in German Literature and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Studies of a major author, a prominent theme in German literature or culture, a movement, or a genre. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Special Topics in German Literature and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Studies of a major author, a prominent theme. Topics vary-for example, the fairy tale, Germanic mythology.

title: Migration in the German Past and Present: Gastarbeiter, Refugees, Displaced Persons (1 Unit)
description: Explores how migration from and to Germany has impacted and shaped the country's political, social, and cultural development as an increasingly diverse country.

title: Cultural History of Beer and Brewing from Germany to Chicago (1 Unit)
description: This course provides an overview of the history and science of beer and brewing in both Germany and Chicago, from the Middle Ages to today. Students will read fictional and philosophical interpretations of beer and its cultural impact. Both a tasting of non-alcoholic malted beverages and a tour of a local brewery will be part of the curriculum.

title: Introduction to Yiddish Culture: Images of the Shtetl (1 Unit)
description: Analysis and discussion of the literary, visual, and filmic images of the communal life developed by Eastern European Jews and inseparably associated with them.

title: Luther and the West (1 Unit)
description: Examination of Luther's work in the context of his life and times. Introduces basic dimensions of Western thought, showing how theology relates to broader cultural, political, social, and aesthetic issues.

title: Advanced Creative Expression in German speaking (1 Unit)
description: A course to improve German listening and speaking skills to the advanced level. Uses current cultural texts, films, and television broadcasts.

title: Advanced Creative Expression in German writing (1 Unit)
description: Practice of advanced and sophisticated structures of written German through a series of linguistic exercises. Readings may vary.

title: German Mass Media: from broadcast to stream (1 Unit)
description: "Discussion of history of German media and journalistic differences. Topics include current events in newspapers, magazines, Internet sources, and news broadcasts.

title: The German Market and the Globalized Economy (1 Unit)
description: Germany's economy, its current problems, business practices, and differences from the United States.

title: Germany, Inc.: Marketing and Corporate Social Responsibility (1 Unit)
description: Students gain skills to function in a multitude of German business contexts, such as management and marketing. They also increase their cross-cultural knowledge and intercultural competency.

title: German Literature 1800-1900: The Age of Revolution (1 Unit)
description: Discussion of key texts in German intellectual history from the Enlightenment to the prerevolutionary period in the 1830s.

title: German Contributions to World Literature (1 Unit)
description: Topics vary-for example, Rilke's poetry; Nietzsche's influence on literature; Thomas Mann; Hesse, the German novel, and the mystic tradition; German intellectual history.

title: German Literature 1900-1945: Myth and Modernity (1 Unit)
description: Literature and thought, events, and ideologies that shaped German cultural, political, and social life from 1900 to 1945, during the Weimar Republic and the Nazi state.

title: German Literature: 1945-1989: Recoveries and Transitions (1 Unit)
description: Examination of the relationship of literature and film to the sociopolitical sphere since 1945.

title: The German Avant-Garde and the Culture of Modernism (1 Unit)
description: Literary and artistic reactions to the impact of modernity, war, and revolution and on the individual and collective experience in Berlin from 1910 to 1920.

title: Prague: City of Cultures, City of Conflict (1 Unit)
description: Examination of the cultural, political, and social transformation of Prague from the 19th century to the present. Cosmopolitan Prague, communist Prague, and capitalist Prague. SLAVIC 328-0 and GERMAN 328-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Shattered Worlds: Representation after the Shoa (1 Unit)
description: Examination of the role of German literature and art in the creation of historical consciousness in the postwar period.

title: Literature of the Cold War (1 Unit)
description: Study of the literature and culture of the German Democratic Republic within social, political, and historical contexts.

title: Writers and their Critics (1 Unit)
description: Study of the texts of leading writers in German through a discussion of the criticism these texts have evoked. Emphasis on 20th and 21st century criticism.

title: Minority Voices in Germany (1 Unit)
description: Study of minority literatures in Germany (including Turkish, Italian, Afro German, and Jewish) within social, political, and historical contexts.

title: Science and Culture in Germany (1 Unit)
description: Exploration of key texts popularizing major scientific innovations in Germany. The focus is on tracing the scientific, political, philosophical, aesthetic history of German as a "green nation" from the 18th century until today.

title: German History: Weimar and Nazi Germany (1 Unit)
description: Survey of German political, economic, social, intellectual, and diplomatic history covering Weimar and Nazi Germany.

title: German History: Germany Since 1945 (1 Unit)
description: Survey of German political, economic, social, intellectual, and diplomatic history covering Germany beginning in 1945 to reunification at the end of the Cold War.

title: Topics in German Literature and Culture (1 Unit)
description: In-depth study of topics in German literature and/or pivotal periods in German culture.

title: Topics in German Literature and Culture (1 Unit)
description: In-depth study of topics in German literature and/or pivotal periods in German culture.

title: The Holocaust (1 Unit)
description: Origins and development of the genocide of European Jewry during World War II. HISTORY 349-0 , GERMAN 349-0 , and JWSH_ST 349-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for more than one of these courses.

title: Yiddish Culture and the Holocaust (1 Unit)
description: Analysis of modern Yiddish literature before the Holocaust as well as literary works that emerged from Yiddish-speaking writers who survived the Second World War.

title: Undergraduate Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Advanced work through supervised reading, research, and discussion.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Open to outstanding German majors with senior standing.

title: Accelerated Elementary Ancient and Biblical Greek (1 Unit)
description: This is the first in an accelerated two-quarter series designed to teach students to read ancient Greek texts, from the biblical New Testament to Homeric poetry and Platonic philosophy. These two quarters will teach all the fundamentals of grammar and vocabulary and lead students directly into a course dedicated to reading the Greek New Testament, and classical Greek texts thereafter. Usually taught in the Winter.

title: Accelerated Elementary Ancient and Biblical Greek (1 Unit)
description: This is the second in an accelerated two-quarter series designed to teach students to read ancient Greek texts, from the biblical New Testament to Homeric poetry and Platonic philosophy. These two quarters will teach all the fundamentals of grammar and vocabulary and lead students directly into a course dedicated to reading the Greek New Testament, and classical Greek texts thereafter. Usually taught in the Spring.

title: Introduction to Greek Literature (1 Unit)
description: Review of basic grammar and vocabulary. Representative selections from Greek authors in their historical and cultural contexts.

title: Introduction to Greek Literature (1 Unit)
description: Review of basic grammar and vocabulary. Representative selections from Greek authors in their historical and cultural contexts.

title: Introduction to Greek Literature (1 Unit)
description: Review of basic grammar and vocabulary. Representative selections from Greek authors in their historical and cultural contexts.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Individual program of study under the direction of a faculty member. For advanced students only. Permission of department required.

title: Coming of Age and Growing Old in the 21st Century (1 Unit)
description: This course introduces students to sociological and life course perspectives on adulthood and aging. It focuses on how sociohistorical context, social location or category, and early life conditions influence individuals' pathways to adulthood and old age.

title: Special Topics in Human Development in Context (1 Unit)
description: Foundational work on special topics.

title: Life 101: Literature and Psychology (1 Unit)
description: A conversation among disciplines, especially psychology and literary studies. Explores how people tell stories to develop a deeper and more multifaceted understanding of life narrative and human life. SLAVIC 262-0 and HDC 262-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.

title: Identity and Motivation (1 Unit)
description: Examines the connection between conceptions of the self and goal-oriented motivation, with particular attention to the influence of social, structural, and cultural forces.

title: Emotional Mysteries (1 Unit)
description: What is an emotion? What happens in our bodies when an emotion is triggered? How can emotions help us live productive, healthy, and connected lives? And can we ever truly understand what somebody else is feeling? These are some of the mysteries that we will seek to unravel in this course.

title: Team Dynamics (1 Unit)
description: In this course, we will explore team dynamics, those forces that influence a team's behavior and performance, and what can enhance or hinder potential for impact. We will analyze the contributors to team functioning and their interrelationships at multiple levels: intrapersonal, interpersonal, group and organizational. Taught with LOC 309-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: The Art and Science of Aging (1 Unit)
description: What does it feel like to move through the adult years and toward "old age"?  How do people's personalities, social relationships, and overall world view change as they grow older?  What does psychological and social science have to say about general trends, as well as individual differences, in aging?  This discussion-based and writing-intensive seminar is sequentially organized in terms of five cardinal themes:  (1) the social/emotional world, (2) generations, (3) memory and the self,  (4) loss, and (5) wisdom of the ages.

title: Identities, Intersections, and Organizations (1 Unit)
description: Developing a robust understanding of how to identify and deconstruct the dynamic and varying role that identities play in organizations is key to understanding how/why organizations behave, whose needs they serve/who is left out or marginalized, and the possibility of/conditions for change. Combined with HDC 315-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses. Students may not enroll in they have received credit for HDC 351-0 or LOC 351-0 .

title: Adolescent Stress: Sources and Solutions (1 Unit)
description: Why are adolescence and early adulthood stressful periods of life? Are they more stressful now than in the past? How do we best define and measure adolescent and young adult stress? This course is an advanced, interactive, undergraduate class in which the instructor and students explore the set of above questions together, through readings, discussions, and through qualitative and quantitative coding and analysis of datasets on adolescent stress.

title: The Myths and Facts of Adolescence (1 Unit)
description: This course delves into the complex and often misunderstood stage of human development, where myths and realities intertwine to shape our understanding of youth while uncovering the scientific truths behind them. This course critically analyzes a broad spectrum of theories, research findings, and real-world applications related to adolescent development.

title: Building Loving and Lasting Relationships: Marriage 101 (1 Unit)
description: The intricacies and problems of close, committed, interpersonal relationships, especially marriage. Juniors and Seniors only.

title: Mapping and Spatial Analysis for Social Issues (1 Unit)
description: The focus of the course is on using the power of geospatial analysis to identify, understand, and make recommendations about addressing social, racial, and other inequities, including access to healthy food, environmental pollution, policing, etc. Students will learn basic use of the ArcGIS online program to support geospatial analysis. Taught with LOC 347-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Special Topics in Human Development in Context (1 Unit)
description: Advanced work on special topics.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: SEE DEPT FOR SECTION AND PERMISSION NUMBERS.

title: Hebrew I (1 Unit)
description: This sequence offers students a systematic introduction to Hebrew language and culture. Emphasizes the four modalities-speaking, listening comprehension, reading, and writing.

title: Hebrew I (1 Unit)
description: This sequence offers students a systematic introduction to Hebrew language and culture. Emphasizes the four modalities-speaking, listening comprehension, reading, and writing.

title: Hebrew I (1 Unit)
description: This sequence offers students a systematic introduction to Hebrew language and culture. Emphasizes the four modalities-speaking, listening comprehension, reading, and writing.

title: Hebrew II (1 Unit)
description: This sequence further develops speaking, listening comprehension, reading, and writing skills in Hebrew. Offers insights into Hebrew culture and history through written and audiovisual materials.

title: Hebrew II (1 Unit)
description: This sequence further develops speaking, listening comprehension, reading, and writing skills in Hebrew. Offers insights into Hebrew culture and history through written and audiovisual materials.

title: Hebrew II (1 Unit)
description: This sequence further develops speaking, listening comprehension, reading, and writing skills in Hebrew. Offers insights into Hebrew culture and history through written and audiovisual materials.

title: Hebrew III: Language and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to and practice of intermediate and advanced grammatical concepts in Hebrew through authentic cultural texts and current media sources. The course focuses on speaking, listening comprehension, reading, and writing skills.

title: Hebrew III: Topics in Hebrew Literature (1 Unit)
description: Hebrew language, literature, and culture. Material includes authentic literary pieces and excerpts and audiovisual material such as films and TV shows. Review of more complex grammar including the development of reading, writing, and speaking skills.

title: Current Events in Israel: Israeli Society through Online News Media (1 Unit)
description: This course focuses on current events in Israel through in-depth exploration of online news media sources such as foreign newspaper articles and videos. Students will gain respect for alternative ideas and diversity of views and learn how news and public opinion are chosen, disseminated, shared. The course focuses on reading, listening, discussion and on building a comprehensive Hebrew vocabulary.

title: Hebrew IV: Advanced Topics in Hebrew Literature (1 Unit)
description: Reading 20th-century Hebrew literature. Presentations, discussion, and essays in Hebrew.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: For students who have advanced with distinction beyond the regular course offerings in Hebrew.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: New Introductory Courses in History (1 Unit)
description: Introductory lecture courses on topics not covered in regular offerings. Content varies. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Europe in the Medieval and Early Modern World (1 Unit)
description: This course introduces majors and non-majors to the history of Europe from roughly 1000-1800. The eight centuries between the medieval Crusades and the Age of Atlantic Revolutions profoundly changed the course of Europe’s history, and the world’s. Topics include: the Crusades, the Black Death, the Renaissance, the Reformations, the Scientific Revolution, colonialism, slavery, and the origins of capitalism.

title: Europe in the Modern World (1 Unit)
description: This course examines the years from 1750 onward, when European events determined the course of world history. We will ask why Europe came to dominate the globe, considering especially industrialization, revolution, and imperial expansion. We will investigate, too, how these apparent triumphs paved the way for subsequent catastrophes, including the world wars, the Holocaust, violent decolonization, and the Cold War.

title: Jewish History I: 750-1492 (1 Unit)
description: Surveys the development of Jewish culture and civilization in the medieval period, and traces the ways in which Jewish culture, thought, and socio-political life developed in dialogue with contemporaneous Christian and Islamic societies. Goals include grappling with how historians use primary documents to reconstruct Jewish history, and learning to read works of historical interpretation with a careful and critical eye. HISTORY 203-1 taught with JWSH_ST 203-1 ; may not receive credit for both.

title: Jewish History II: Early Modern, 1492-1789 (1 Unit)
description: Jewish community's economic and cultural reshaping; legalized readmission of Jews to European cities and integration into European society. HISTORY 203-2 taught with JWSH_ST 203-2 ; may not receive credit for both.

title: Jewish History III: 1789-1948 (1 Unit)
description: Plurality of models of integration, acculturation, and assimilation; multiple identities; split of traditional community; sociocultural behavior; political movements. HISTORY 203-3 taught with JWSH_ST 203-3 ; may not receive credit for both.

title: North America and the United States to 1865 (1 Unit)
description: The setting: North America, 1400 to 1865. The characters: Indigenous, African, European, Asian, and their descendants; enslaved, free, and in-between; genders of all kinds. The plot: full of tragedy and triumph and terror and twists, including the surprising emergence of the United States, the world’s first modern republican empire, trumpeting inalienable rights and the pursuit of happiness while spreading slavery.

title: History of the United States, Reconstruction to the Present (1 Unit)
description: Interpretative survey from the 17th century to the present. Reconstruction to the present. Lectures, discussion sections.

title: American Wars (1 Unit)
description: This new lecture course views the United States, its peoples, and its place in the world through the prism of war. It begins in colonial North America and continues to the present day. No prerequisites or prior knowledge are required; all students are welcome.

title: Introduction to African-American History: Key concepts from 1700-1861 (1 Unit)
description: African origins, the slave trade, origins of slavery and racism in the United States, life under slavery in the North and the South. BLK_ST and HISTORY 212-1 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.
