description: Nuclei and their constituents; nuclear models; alpha and beta decay; nuclear fission and fusion; the strong, electromagnetic, and weak interactions; and the fundamental particles and particle schemes.

title: Introduction to General Relativity (1 Unit)
description: Review of special relativity and Newtonian gravity; curved space-time; geodesics and conservation laws; Schwarzschild geometry; tests of general relativity; black holes; linearized gravity and gravitational waves; and big bang cosmology.

title: Introduction to Computational Physics (1 Unit)
description: Application of computing to physics: Monte Carlo simulation, numerical integration of equations of motion, discrete element methods in electromagnetism.

title: Optics Laboratory (1 Unit)
description: Optics/laser lab focusing on optical instruments widely used in medical/biological studies, including optical microscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, tumor detection in optical scattering, and optical fibers in endoscopes.

title: Electronics (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to modern electronics, construction of elementary analog and digital circuits.

title: Advanced Physics Laboratory (1 Unit)
description: Modern experimental techniques and data analysis methods.  Both classic and modern experiments in atomic/nuclear physics, electricity and magnetism, optics, condensed matter physics, and nonlinear dynamics.  This laboratory emphasizes independent work. This course consists primarily of two four-hour sessions per week, which may have lecture and laboratory periods.

title: Classical Optics and Special Relativity (1 Unit)
description: Advanced topics following from electrodynamics, including advanced classical optics, Fraunhofer and Fresnel diffraction, radiation from accelerated charges, wave guides and/or antennae, and special relativity, including dynamics.

title: Nonlinear Dynamics and Chaos (1 Unit)
description: This course covers the mathematics of nonlinear oscillations, fractal geometry, chaotic dynamics, the dynamics of complex systems, and physics applications of these ideas. Projects involving applications of nonlinear dynamics and chaos are integral to this course.

title: Independent Thesis Research (1-2 Units)
description: Individual study under the direction of a faculty member. Open only to advanced students pursuing departmental honors.

title: Independent Study (1-2 Units)
description: Opportunity to study an advanced subject of interest under the individual direction of a faculty member. Open to all advanced students; consent of instructor required.

title: Technique Class - First Year (0 Unit)
description: Introductory piano techniques for first year piano performance majors.

title: Applied Piano for Music Majors (1 Unit)
Technique Class - Second Year (0 Unit)
description: Intermediate piano techniques for second year piano performance majors.

title: Applied Piano for Music Majors (1 Unit)
Piano Repertoire I (1 Unit)
description: Analytical and historical study of piano solo and concerto repertoire from early keyboard literature to the present.

title: Piano Repertoire II (1 Unit)
description: Analytical and historical study of piano solo and concerto repertoire from early keyboard literature to the present.

title: Piano Repertoire III (1 Unit)
description: Analytical and historical study of piano solo and concerto repertoire from early keyboard literature to the present.

title: Piano Pedagogy I (0.5 Unit)
description: Lecture/demonstration/laboratory course in piano teaching at all levels. Principles and techniques of group and individual instruction; survey of teaching materials. Seniors and graduate students.

title: Piano Pedagogy II (0.5 Unit)
description: Lecture/demonstration/laboratory course in piano teaching at all levels. Principles and techniques of group and individual instruction; survey of teaching materials. Seniors and graduate students.

title: Piano Pedagogy III (0.5 Unit)
description: Lecture/demonstration/laboratory course in piano teaching at all levels. Principles and techniques of group and individual instruction; survey of teaching materials. Seniors and graduate students.

title: Collaborative Piano-Beginning I (0.5 Unit)
description: Piano students work with a singer and instrumentalist in the preparation and performance of mainstream recital repertoire.

title: Collaborative Piano-Beginning II (0.5 Unit)
description: Piano students work with a singer and instrumentalist in the preparation and performance of mainstream recital repertoire.

title: Collaborative Piano-Beginning III (0.5 Unit)
description: Piano students work with a singer and instrumentalist in the preparation and performance of mainstream recital repertoire.

title: Duo Sonata Class (0.5 Unit)
description: Provides an opportunity for pianists and their vocal and/or instrumental partners to receive intensive coaching on works of their choice. A final performance is required.

title: Selected Topics (1 Unit)
description: Topics vary; announced before registration. May be repeated.

title: Applied Keyboard for Music Majors (0.5 Unit)
Applied Piano for Music Majors (1 Unit)
Studio Ensemble for Music Majors (0.5 Unit)
Repertoire Studies (0.5 Unit)
description: Topics vary by quarter.

title: Elementary Polish (1 Unit)
description: First course in a three-quarter sequence in elementary Polish. Communicative approaches to reading, writing, listening, and speaking. No prerequisite.

title: Elementary Polish (1 Unit)
description: Second course in a three-quarter sequence in elementary polish. Communicative approaches to reading, writing, listening, and speaking.

title: Elementary Polish (1 Unit)
description: Third course in a three-quarter sequence in elementary Polish. Communicative approaches to reading, writing, listening, and speaking.

title: Intermediate Polish: Language and Culture (1 Unit)
description: First course in a three-quarter sequence in intermediate Polish. Further development of reading, writing, speaking and listening proficiency through communicative approaches. Continued focus on Polish culture and society.

title: Intermediate Polish: Language and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Second course in a three-quarter sequence in intermediate Polish. Further development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening proficiency through communicative approaches. Continued focus on topics in Polish culture and society.

title: Intermediate Polish: Language and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Third course in a three-quarter sequence in intermediate Polish. Further development of reading, writing, speaking, and listening proficiency through communicative approaches. Continued focus on topics in Polish culture and society.

title: Polish for Advanced and Native Speakers (1 Unit)
description: Polish for advanced speakers, including heritage speakers. Stress on advanced levels of reading and writing as well as speaking. Taught entirely in Polish. Content varies; may be repeated for credit.

title: Polish for Advanced and Native Speakers (1 Unit)
description: Polish for advanced speakers, including heritage speakers. Stress on advanced levels of reading and writing as well as speaking. Taught entirely in Polish. Content varies; may be repeated for credit.

title: Elementary Portuguese (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to grammar and development of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills in Brazilian Portuguese, as well as the history and culture of Portuguese-speaking countries.

title: Elementary Portuguese (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to grammar and development of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills in Brazilian Portuguese, as well as the history and culture of Portuguese-speaking countries.

title: Elementary Portuguese (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to grammar and development of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills in Brazilian Portuguese, as well as the history and culture of Portuguese-speaking countries.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Portuguese for Speakers of Spanish and other Romance Languages (1 Unit)
description: For students proficient in Spanish, French or Italian. Comparative sociolinguistic and interactive approach to communicative competence emphasizing pronunciation, intonation, sentence structure, and patterns of spoken and written Portuguese.

title: Portuguese for Speakers of Spanish and other Romance Languages (1 Unit)
description: For students proficient in Spanish, French or Italian. Comparative sociolinguistic and interactive approach to communicative competence emphasizing pronunciation, intonation, sentence structure, and patterns of spoken and written Portuguese.

title: Intermediate Portuguese (1 Unit)
description: Port 121 helps students achieve an intermediate level of proficiency through further development of listening, speaking, reading and writing skills. Grammar, vocabulary and pronunciation of Brazilian Portuguese will continue to be developed through meaningful cultural contexts. The course also offers insights into the history and culture of the Portuguese speaking countries in Europe, Africa and America.

title: Intermediate Portuguese (1 Unit)
description: Port 121 helps students achieve an intermediate level of proficiency through further development of listening, speaking, reading and writing skills. Grammar, vocabulary and pronunciation of Brazilian Portuguese will continue to be developed through meaningful cultural contexts. The course also offers insights into the history and culture of the Portuguese speaking countries in Europe, Africa and America.

title: Intermediate Portuguese (1 Unit)
description: Port 121 helps students achieve an intermediate level of proficiency through further development of listening, speaking, reading and writing skills. Grammar, vocabulary and pronunciation of Brazilian Portuguese will continue to be developed through meaningful cultural contexts. The course also offers insights into the history and culture of the Portuguese speaking countries in Europe, Africa and America.

title: Reading and Speaking Portuguese (1 Unit)
description: This intermediate course is designed to expand mastery in reading and speaking Brazilian Portuguese through select cultural videos, readings of literary cronicas, periodicals, and the Internet.

title: Reading and Writing Portuguese (1 Unit)
description: Instruction in reading and writing expository and narrative prose. Emphasis on vocabulary, linguistic skills, and syntax appropriate to formal written Portuguese.

title: Icons, Legends, and Myths in Brazil (1 Unit)
description: Representations in graphic materials, documentaries, film, theater, folklore, narrative fiction, and popular music of historical, literary, and popular figures in the national imagination. May include English or Portuguese discussion sections. Prerequisite for Portuguese section: PORT 201-0 , PORT 202-0 , or sufficient score on placement exam. Prerequisite for English section: none.

title: Introductory Topics in Luso-Afro-Brazilian Studies (0 Unit)
description: Introduction to the histories and cultures of the Lusophone world (Brazil, Portugal, Lusophone Africa and/or Asia) through literature, graphic novels, documentaries, film, theater, folklore, and/or popular music. Content may span the precolonial/premodern, colonial, and postcolonial periods. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Topics in Advanced Portuguese (1 Unit)
description: Advanced review of grammar concepts and idiomatic use of spoken and written Portuguese. Deals with a variety of topics in the context of Brazilian and Lusophone culture, history, literature, and current events. May be taken more than once for credit with change of topic.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Independent study under supervision. Consultation with the director of undergraduate studies required.

title: McCormick First-Year Experience (0 Unit)
description: A series of peer-led small group discussions for first-year engineering students, covering topics like higher education culture, time management, strategies for success in courses, and mindset. Attendance is required.

title: McCormick First-Year Experience (0 Unit)
description: A series of peer-led small group discussions for first-year engineering students covering topics such as academic strategies, choice of major, exploration of non-major options, and health and well-being.  Attendance is required.

title: PATH (Personal Development Studio Lab) (0 Unit)
description: PATH explores opportunities to improve performance on many different levels. Students will begin with a values assessment and set short term goals, then explore the role of attention, mindset, learning science, time management and self-compassion in achieving personal, academic, and future professional success. PATH is a prerequisite for the Curious Life Certificate.

title: Designing Your Life (1 Unit)
description: Considers an approach to life as a series of design projects to help students craft a total life. Includes seminar-style discussions, role-playing, short writing assignments, hands-on making, guest speakers, and individual mentoring and coaching.

title: Emotional Intelligence 101 - Managing Yourself, Maximizing Your Potential (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to emotional intelligence theories and concepts; provides practical tools for building skills in stress management, intrapersonal and interpersonal awareness, peak performance, resilience/adaptability, and general mood.

title: Engineering Improv I: The Art of Allowing (0.5 Unit)
description: Start anywhere, remember you are not the most important person in the scene, and say yes.  Through these and other improv techniques, students learn to tackle unexpected obstacles, building skills that can be leveraged in both academic and non-academic contexts to face challenges with resilience.

title: Engineering Improv II: The Art of Application (0.5 Unit)
description: This course builds on Improv I and takes students deeper into concepts and applications.

title: Whole-Body Thinking: Collaborative Problem Solving through Partner Dancing (1 Unit)
description: In this approach to swing dancing, the goal is for two people to join hands and use the rhythms they hear in swinging, jazz-rhythm-based music as a means of connecting with each other.

title: Yoga Foundations: Living a Life of Service, Discipline, and Joy (1 Unit)
description: Explore the intersection of yoga principles and personal development with topics such as living your dharma, the guiding principles of yoga, courage before confidence, and the discipline of joy. Learn how to live a more peaceful, meaningful, purpose-driven life based on the tenants of yoga. The course is part lecture, part physical practice.

title: Special Topics in Personal Development (1 Unit)
description: Topics suggested by students or faculty and approved by the curriculum committee.

title: Topics in Personal Development (0 Unit)
description: Topics of limited scope as suggested by faculty or students and approved by the McCormick Office of Personal Development.

title: Selected Topics in Personal Development (0.5 Unit)
description: Topics of limited scope as suggested by faculty or students and approved by the McCormick Curriculum Committee.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Indigenous Summer Research Program (0 Unit)
description: The Indigenous Summer Research Program (ISRP) is an 8-week residential internship, training the next generation of social and cultural psychologists. Application is open to students with demonstrated prior commitment to Indigenous Studies. Participants work with RISE staff and faculty to develop a research project investigating Indigenous issues. At the program's conclusion, interns present their work at the ISRP Conference. Prerequiste: Admission to program.

title: Introduction to Psychology (1 Unit)
description: A survey course reviewing primary psychological research and theories of human behavior. Laboratory experience exposes students to psychology as a research science.

title: Statistical Methods in Psychology (1 Unit)
description: Measurement; descriptive statistics; probability and sampling; T-test, ANOVA, correlation, and regression.

title: Social Psychology (1 Unit)
description: Psychological processes underlying social behavior; topics include social cognition, attraction, aggression, prejudice, and behavior in groups.

title: Psychology of Personality (1 Unit)
description: Nature of personality and its development. Modern theoretical interpretations. Biological and social bases of individual differences.

title: Introduction to Neuroscience (1 Unit)
description: Designed for students with no prior coursework in neuroscience or biology. Neurophysiology and neuroanatomy; neuroscience of perception, emotion, morality, memory, mental illness, and consciousness. Neuroscience majors may not take PSYCH 221-0 .  Students may not take PSYCH 221-0 after they have taken NEUROSCI 202-0 or NEUROSCI 206-0 .

title: Cognitive Psychology (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to research into mental processes such as perception, memory, reasoning, and decision making.

title: Developmental Psychology (1 Unit)
description: Development of cognitive, social, and other psychological functions.

title: Health Psychology (1 Unit)
description: Overview of research in health psychology. Stress and coping, biological systems affected by stress, social support and health, health behaviors, adjustment to chronic illnesses.

title: Buddhist Psychology (1 Unit)
description: Buddhist and scientific psychological views of mind and behavior; meditation techniques.

title: Research Methods in Psychology (1 Unit)
description: Methods of psychological research; experimental design; reliability and validity; review and application of statistics; execution and reporting of psychological research.

title: Introduction to Clinical Psychology (1 Unit)
description: Definition and history of clinical psychology, personality theory in clinical psychology, diagnosis and classification of disorders, assessment, psychotherapy, and ethical issues.

title: Cognitive Behavior Therapy (1 Unit)
description: Scientific foundations of cognitive behavior therapy for a wide range of disorders. Focus on the rationale for different treatments and evidence of efficacy and process. Comparisons with other scientifically validated treatments.

title: Special Topics in Social/Clinical/Personality (1 Unit)
description: Topic to be announced. Prerequisites vary. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Psychology of Attitudes (1 Unit)
description: Survey of social psychological research on attitudes; focus on the formation of attitudes, the relationship between attitudes and behavior, and attitude change.

title: Relationship Science (1 Unit)
description: Social-psychological analysis of close relationships, with an emphasis on romantic relationships. Interpersonal processes associated with relationship formation, development, and dissolution.

title: The Holocaust: Psychological Themes & Perspectives (1 Unit)
description: Exploration of how particular psychological theories and concepts can inform understanding of the Holocaust at group and individual levels. Social and clinical psychology are emphasized.

title: Brain and Cognition (1 Unit)
description: Neural bases of cognitive processing with emphases on neuroimaging approaches in the areas of encoding, perception, attention, memory, language, reading, motor control, and executive functioning.  Taught with CSD 303-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Brain Damage and the Mind (1 Unit)
description: Survey of human cognition as studied via investigations of brain damage and brain-imaging techniques. Mandatory.

title: Special Topics in Cognition/Neuroscience (1 Unit)
description: Topic to be announced. Prerequisites vary. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Consciousness (1 Unit)
description: Examines how psychologists, neuroscientists, computer scientists, and physicists have tackled fundamental questions about consciousness using empirical and theoretical methods.

title: Positive Psychology: The Science of Well-Being (1 Unit)
description: Key developments in the field of positive psychology. Eudaimonic and hedonic wellbeing, mindfulness and flow, importance of social connections, emotional resilience, positive institutions.

title: Psychology of Beauty (1 Unit)
description: Theory, methodology, and empirical data related to the psychological impact of human beauty. Emphasis on both cultural and evolutionary perspectives.

title: Cultural Psychology (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to concepts and empirical methods used to study how culture shapes mind, brain, and behavior over multiple time scales, including over generations and the lifespan and across situational contexts.

title: Presenting Ideas & Data (1 Unit)
description: Understanding principles of cognitive psychology, data visualization, and graphic design to present ideas and data in an engaging, clear, and memorable manner. PSYCH 345-0 and COG_SCI 345-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Psychology of Sex and Gender Differences (1 Unit)
description: Examination of sex differences and similarities. Evaluation of explanations for differences. Review of how gender affects achievement, relationships, and mental health.

title: Personality Research (1 Unit)
description: Research in personality, with emphasis on experimental approaches and methods. Basic concepts of test reliability and validity. Students conduct original research.

title: Social and Personality Development (1 Unit)
description: Research methods, theories, and facts relating to the development and modification of attitudes and behavior.

title: Stereotyping & Prejudice (1 Unit)
description: Analysis of the causes and consequences of stereotyping and prejudice, as well as methods used to study these issues. Students conduct original research.

title: Child Psychopathology (1 Unit)
description: Major forms of psychopathology present during childhood, including disorders exclusive to childhood and those that may appear during any developmental period. Developmental models of the etiology and course of major psychopathologies.

title: Psychological Tests & Measures (1 Unit)
description: Explores the science of psychological assessment, including its history, test construction and evaluation, and common measures of personality, psychopathology, and ability. Students create and evaluate their own psychological measures.

title: Cognitive Development (1 Unit)
description: Cognitive development in infancy, childhood, and adolescence. Focus on theoretical explanations for cognitive change and development in core domains, including language, space, number, time, and social relations.

title: Language and Cognition (1 Unit)
description: Exposure to original research and theoretical perspectives on language and its relation to thought and behavior. Critical analysis of theories and methods. Topics may vary.

title: Human Memory (1 Unit)
description: Scientific study of human memory, including memory systems of the brain, amnesia, remembering, forgetting, encoding, consolidation, memory suppression, and memory distortion. Emphasizes original research reports in cognitive neuroscience.  Prerequisites: PSYCH 301-0 and PSYCH 228, 328, or 336.

title: Advanced Statistics & Experimental Design (1 Unit)
description: Advanced topics in research design and analysis of data. Focus on both theory and applications.

title: Children & the Law (1 Unit)
description: Examines research on children's involvement in the legal system as decision makers, witnesses, victims, and perpetrators from a developmental perspective . Taught with LEGAL_ST 381-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Psychology and Food (1 Unit)
description: Social, cultural, cognitive, evolutionary, and biological factors that influence food choice and consumption. Conducting and evaluating research on eating behavior. Students conduct original research.

title: Consumer Psychology and Marketing Research (1 Unit)
description: Application of psychological theories, findings, and methodologies to marketing research questions and problems. Students conduct a marketing research project for an actual client.

title: Advanced Seminar in Cognition or Neuroscience (1 Unit)
description: Discussion and critical analysis of research methods and findings in an area of cognitive psychology and/or neuroscience. Topics vary. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Advanced Seminar in Psychology (1 Unit)
description: Discussion and critical analysis of research methods and findings in psychology. Interdisciplinary focus, often spanning natural and social science aspects of psychology. Topics vary. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Advanced Supervised Research 1 (1 Unit)
description: Design, implementation, and reporting of a psychology research project.

title: Advanced Supervised Research 2 (1 Unit)
description: Design, implementation, and reporting of a psychology research project.

title: Senior Thesis Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Open only to students pursuing departmental honors. They must apply for admission in spring quarter of junior year.

title: Senior Thesis Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Open only to students pursuing departmental honors. They must apply for admission in spring quarter of junior year.

title: Senior Thesis Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Open only to students pursuing departmental honors. They must apply for admission in spring quarter of junior year.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Consent of instructor required. Generally limited to juniors and seniors.  See department requirements for eligibility.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Introduction to the Study of Religion (1 Unit)
description: Introduces students to foundational themes, categories, content, and modes of analysis for studying religion.

title: Introduction to Religion, Media, and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Integrating multimedia modes of learning and engaging students in skill-developing mediamaking assignments, this course offers undergraduates an introduction to studying the phenomena of religion in relationship to dynamics of media, society, and culture. No prerequisites required.

title: Religion, Medicine & Suffering in the West (1 Unit)
description: Examination of religious healing ceremonies and Christian perspectives on pain and suffering in light of the meaning of physical pain in people's everyday lives.

title: Introduction to Hinduism (1 Unit)
description: Unity and diversity of Hindu mythology, beliefs, and practices from ancient times to the present.

title: Introduction to Buddhism (1 Unit)
description: The Buddha's life and teachings, traditions that developed from these teachings, and systems of meditation, rituals, and ethics.

title: Introduction to Hebrew Bible (1 Unit)
description: Critical introduction to the Hebrew Bible: its history, content, and cultural influence, as well as the sociopolitical history of ancient Israel. RELIGION 220-0 and JWSH_ST 220-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.

title: Introduction to the New Testament (1 Unit)
description: Beginning, development, and content of the New Testament; its Jewish and Hellenistic environment.

title: Introduction to Judaism (1 Unit)
description: Main concepts in the theology of Judaism, main rituals and customs, and main institutions. RELIGION 230-0 and JWSH_ST 230-0 taught together; may not receive credit for both.

title: Introduction to Christianity (1 Unit)
description: Doctrine, worship, and institutions in the various branches of Christianity.

title: Introduction to Islam (1 Unit)
description: Principal beliefs and practices of Muslims set against the historic development of the faith.

title: Topics in Religion, Nature, and Ecology (1 Unit)
description: Explores the relationships between religion, nature, and ecology in text and practices across various traditions and cultural contexts. Topics may include environmental ethics, nature mysticism, religious conservation movements, environmental consciousness, and the role of spirituality in ecological activism. May be taken multiple times with different content.

title: Introduction to Black Religions: The North American Experience (1 Unit)
description: Introduces students to the variety of Black religions that developed during and after the Atlantic slave trade up to the present. Explores these traditions as continuities/changes of West African religious cosmologies. Examines the interplay between religion, politics, and the constructions of racial identities within various forms of Christianity, Islam, and other expressive cultures. RELIGION 262-0 and BLK_ST 262-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.

title: American Religious History from 1865 to the Great Depression (1 Unit)
description: Topics include urban religion, African American churches, Christians and foreign policy, immigrant religion, the spiritual crisis of the 1920s, and Pentecostalism.

title: American Religious History from World War II to the Present (1 Unit)
description: Religion and the making of contemporary America, including Cold War religion, the Black Gods of the Great Migration, the rise of the Christian Right, and modern American Catholicism and Judaism.

title: Introduction to Theology (1 Unit)
description: Theology as an academic discipline with a long history of asking fundamental questions about religious experience, texts, practices, and ideas in dialogue with thinkers past and present.

title: Theology of Love (1 Unit)
description: The concept of love from theological, historical, philosophical, and biblical perspectives.

title: Luther and the West (1 Unit)
description: Examination of Luther's work in the context of his life and times. Introduces basic dimensions of Western thought, showing how theology relates to broader cultural, political, social, and aesthetic issues. GERMAN 272-0 and RELIGION 272-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Mysticism and Spirituality (1 Unit)
description: Examines mysticism (mystical experience, relationship, prayer, or consciousness) and spirituality (spiritual presence and practice) across religious traditions and outside traditions.

title: Introductory Readings in Sanskrit I (1 Unit)
description: This course is the first of a two quarter sequence that provides instruction in the Sanskrit language for beginners. No prior knowledge is required. It begins a comprehensive introduction to the Sanskrit language, while engaging students from the beginning in the practice of translation.

title: Introductory Readings in Sanskrit II (1 Unit)
description: This course is the second of a two quarter sequence that provides instruction in the Sanskrit language for beginners. Students continue a comprehensive introduction to the Sanskrit language through study of its forms and through translation.

title: Indian Philosophy (1 Unit)
description: This course offers an introduction to ancient Indian philosophical traditions. We will look first at the distinctive  features of philosophy in South Asia, including its particular aims and presuppositions. Thereafter we will look  in some detail at two major Hindu traditions from the classical period: Sāṁkhya-Yoga and Advaita Vedānta.  We also briefly explore the Hindu traditions of Nyāya and Vaiśeṣika. From there, we will turn to look at  classical Buddhist and Jain philosophy. Our major topics will include metaphysics, consciousness, ego,  meditation and the release from human suffering.

title: Topics in Hinduism (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Buddhism in the Contemporary World (1 Unit)
description: An exploration of where, why, and how Buddhist practices, ideas, and iconography are spreading and being reinterpreted in the modern world.

title: Topics in East Asian Religions (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Topics in Buddhism (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Topics in the Bible (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Judaism in the Modern World (1 Unit)
description: Radical changes that emancipation and modernity have brought to the religious expression of Judaism. May be repeated for credit.

title: Idea of Sainthood in Christianity (1 Unit)
description: Historical and contemporary conceptions of sanctity, especially in Roman Catholicism and Eastern Orthodoxy.

title: Church Architecture (1 Unit)
description: Survey of historical and recent churches: spatial dynamics, centering focus, aesthetic impact, and symbolic resonance.

title: Topics in Christianity (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: The Qur'an (1 Unit)
description: Islam's sacred scripture, Muslim understandings of revelation and prophecy, and debates about the Qur'an in the contemporary world.

title: Islamic Law (1 Unit)
description: Evolution of Islamic Law from the Prophet Muhammad to the contemporary world, focusing on the impact of colonialism.

title: Sufism (1 Unit)
description: The historical development of Islamic mystical traditions, with special attention to notions of mystical experience, sainthood, and devotion to the Prophet Muhammad.

title: Topics in Islam (1 Unit)
description: Selected topics in Islamic history and thought. May be repeated for credit with different topic.

title: Black Religions (1 Unit)
description: Exploration of the historical diversity of African American religious experiences and identities.

title: Topics in Women and Religion in America (1 Unit)
description: Topics, figures, events, and dynamics in the history of American women and religion.

title: Topics in American Religion (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Religion, TV, and Film (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. Explores expressions and representations of religion, spirituality, moral questions, transcendence, meaning of life, etc. in TV and film.

title: Religion and Bioethics (1 Unit)
description: Analysis of contemporary dilemmas in medicine and the life sciences; responses to these dilemmas from religious perspectives.

title: Contemporary Religious Thought (1 Unit)
description: Content varies (e.g., convergence between religious paths, science and religion, politics and religion). May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Death: Myth and Meaning (1 Unit)
description: Ideas, beliefs, and practices pertaining to death from a variety of ancient and modern cultures. Immortality, afterlife, care for the dying, suicide, funerary rituals, transhumanism.

title: Topics in Comparative Religion (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit with change of topic.

title: Global Catholicism in the Contemporary World (1 Unit)
description: Historical and contemporary global Catholicism. Topics include the church and political modernity; local saints; controversies over worship styles; Catholics and political revolutions; the Vatican; the pontificate of John Paul II.

title: Religion, Law, & Politics: Politics of Religious Diversity (1 Unit)
description: This course teaches students to think critically, comparatively, and globally about the intersections of religion, law, and politics. It considers how religious, legal, and political traditions intersect and interact in modern states and societies. The course is organized around a set of legal cases and supporting materials in the Teaching Law and Religion Case Study Archive. RELIGION 382-0 taught with POLI_SCI 382-0 ; may not receive credit for both.

title: Topics in United States Catholicism (1 Unit)
description: Historical and contemporary subjects in the study of Catholic culture in the United States. May be taken multiple times with different content.

title: Topics in Latin America Catholicism (1 Unit)
description: Historical and contemporary subjects in the study of Catholic culture in Latin America. May be taken multiple times with different content.

title: Theories of Religion (1 Unit)
description: Ways of critically analyzing religious experience and its meaning. Phenomenology of religion, history of religions, comparative religions. Designed for majors and minors in Religious Studies but all are welcome.

title: Senior Seminar (1 Unit)
description: For honors students writing the senior thesis.

title: Senior Seminar (1 Unit)
description: For honors students writing the senior thesis.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Reading and conferences on special subjects for advanced students. Consent of instructor required.

title: Media Construction (1 Unit)
description: Introduces the core components of media-idea, image, sound, and sequence- with the technical fundamentals involved in shooting and editing video. Students work with SLR and digital video cameras and with Photoshop and Final Cut Pro editing software, completing four projects in different genres during the quarter. Prerequisite for all upper-level production courses. Required for majors; typically taken in first year.

title: First-Year Topics Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Beginning seminar focused around a special topic of media analysis, history, or theory. Students will learn research, analytic, and writing skills while focusing on issues relevant to film, media and/or digital arts and culture.

title: Analyzing Media Texts (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to the study of the moving image. Basic elements of style across media including film, television, and interactive media. Focus on close analysis of texts to find significance. Prerequisite for upper level courses in the department. Required for majors; typically taken in first year.

title: Foundations of Screenwriting (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to writing for the screen (film, television, and/or computer). Structure, character, dialogue, format, voice, scope, pace, context. Lecture/workshop. Prerequisite for upper-level writing courses in the department.

title: Studies in Media Topics (1 Unit)
description: Theoretical or practical or both; emphasis on evolving trends.

title: Introduction to Acting for the Screen (1 Unit)
description: Foundational concerns and practices for screen acting. Scene analysis, rehearsal, staging and camera space, casting, editing for performance. Creating and portraying characters for most effective capture by the camera. Film directing techniques as related to the actor. Required introductory course for the Acting for Screen module sequence.

title: Advanced Acting for Screen (1 Unit)
description: A retrospective critique of curricular and extracurricular performance work in the Acting for Screen module intended for seniors and other students about to graduate. Course will involve continued advanced scene work, as well as an evaluation of performer’s range and capabilities as they apply to film and television industries, and the student’s professional self-presentation through a self-edited actor reel and resume.

title: Topics in Acting for the Screen (1 Unit)
description: Production-based courses on a range of practices and methods in acting for screen. May be repeated as topic varies. Counts as elective for the Acting for Screen module.

title: Practicum in RTVF Research (1 Unit)
description: Collaboration with a faculty member on design and execution of a media research project. Students learn how to construct and complete a research project and document results. Requires a paper or other form of work product as determined by the faculty member.

title: Advanced Directing I (1 Unit)
description: Three-quarter sequence for students creating advanced artistic production, with critique of work throughout the production and post-production process; conceptual resources offered as needed. Students may work in any genre (documentary, narrative, experimental) and any medium. Admission based on portfolio of previous media work and proposal for project, including script and budget.

title: Advanced Directing II (1 Unit)
description: Three-quarter sequence for students creating advanced artistic production, with critique of work throughout the production and post-production process; conceptual resources offered as needed. Students may work in any genre (documentary, narrative, experimental) and any medium. Admission based on portfolio of previous media work and proposal for project, including script and budget.

title: Advanced Directing III (1 Unit)
description: Three-quarter sequence for students creating advanced artistic production, with critique of work throughout the production and post-production process; conceptual resources offered as needed. Students may work in any genre (documentary, narrative, experimental) and any medium. Admission based on portfolio of previous media work and proposal for project, including script and budget.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Prerequisite: consent of undergraduate dean after submission of petition.

title: Elementary Russian (1 Unit)
description: Russian language and culture. Basic reading, writing, listening, and speaking. No prerequisite.

title: Elementary Russian (1 Unit)
description: Russian language and culture. Basic reading, writing, listening, and speaking.

title: Elementary Russian (1 Unit)
description: Russian language and culture. Basic reading, writing, listening, and speaking.

title: Intermediate Russian (1 Unit)
description: Russian language and culture. Reading, writing, listening, and speaking.

title: Intermediate Russian (1 Unit)
description: Russian language and culture. Reading, writing, listening, and speaking.

title: Intermediate Russian (1 Unit)
description: Russian language and culture. Reading, writing, listening, and speaking.

title: Advanced Russian in Conversations (1 Unit)
description: Conversation, listening comprehension, reading, and composition. Contemporary readings on Russian culture and society. Combined third-and fourth-year multi-skill course.

title: Advanced Russian in Conversations (1 Unit)
description: Conversation, listening comprehension, reading, and composition. Contemporary readings on Russian culture and society. Combined third-and fourth-year multi-skill course.

title: Advanced Russian in Conversations (1 Unit)
description: Conversation, listening comprehension, reading, and composition. Contemporary readings on Russian culture and society. Combined third-and fourth-year multi-skill course.

title: Advanced Russian Language and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Conversation, listening comprehension, reading, and composition. Exploration of modern Russian language and culture through readings, video, and film. Combined third-and fourth-year multi-skill course.

title: Advanced Russian Language and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Conversation, listening comprehension, reading, and composition. Exploration of modern Russian language and culture through readings, video, and film. Combined third-and fourth-year multi-skill course.

title: Advanced Russian Language and Culture (1 Unit)
description: Conversation, listening comprehension, reading, and composition. Exploration of modern Russian language and culture through readings, video, and film. Combined third-and fourth-year multi-skill course.

title: Advanced Contemporary Russian (1 Unit)
description: Russian for advanced speakers, including heritage speakers. Stress on skills in speaking, reading, and writing in professional and formal environments. Taught entirely in Russian.

title: Advanced Contemporary Russian (1 Unit)
description: Russian for advanced speakers, including heritage speakers. Stress on skills in speaking, reading, and writing in professional and formal environments.

title: Finding Your Path (0 Unit)
description: A continuation of Finding Your Path: Pathways and Future Possibilities (SESP 251), a course that supports second-year students most impacted by the historical and contemporary realities of classism and racism as they map their path forward to career fulfillment. This class is a 0 credit class in which Pathways students conduct a search for a summer internship and then complete said internship.

title: Civic Engagement 1- Participatory Policymaking (1 Unit)
description: Policy implementation involves more than just identifying and researching options, policymakers must, consider design program design, costs & sustainability; political feasibility, and building political support.  In this class, you will learn how to design policy for implementation through Northwestern’s Participatory Budgeting process.

title: Civic Engagement 2: Participatory Budgeting (1 Unit)
description: Democracy gives limited opportunities for citizens to influence decision-making.  In this class you will learn to implement open democracy innovations, that are more inclusive, more representative, and lead to better policy outcomes, by implementing a campus-wide participatory budgeting process, where community decides how to spend $1000 to address climate change.

title: Civic Engagement 3: Organizing, Gathering & Policy implementation (1 Unit)
description: How do we motivate people to take action? In this class, you will learn the techniques of relational organizing (canvassing, one-on-ones, public narrative), designing civic gatherings (Civic Saturdays) that move people to action to build social movements.  You will also oversee policy implementation of community development projects selected in the participatory budgeting process.

title: Understanding Knowledge (1 Unit)
description: What does it mean to know something? What are the different types of knowledge and what distinguishes them from one another? What counts as fact vs. opinion vs. belief and so on; who gets to decide and under what conditions? How is knowledge produced and how does it gain traction? How does the source and type of knowledge interact with socio-political-cultural constructs and systems of power and, in turn, how can "knowledge" be used to produce and/or perpetuate power and privilege or to empower those who are marginalized?  In this course students will explore these and other questions to gain insight into the social production, distribution, consumption, interpretation, and operationalization of "knowledge."

title: Human Development: Childhood and Adolescence (1 Unit)
description: Personal, social, and cognitive development from birth through adolescence. Interplay of biological and experiential factors on linguistic and conceptual development, ego, and personality.

title: Human Development: Adulthood and Aging (1 Unit)
description: Psychological, sociological, and biological factors influencing socialization and development from young and middle adulthood through old age. Influences of family, school, and work on the individual.

title: Introduction to Statistics and Research Methodology (1 Unit)
description: Definitions and classifications of terms used in quantitative methods; measures of typical and maximum performance, reliability, and validity checks; reporting and displaying data; interpreting results.

title: Leaders Lab (1 Unit)
description: Leaders Lab is an interactive, engaging and dynamic fall quarter course that was created and designed for incoming first and second-year students in SESP to reflect, experience and engage in dialogue about the "big" questions in life such as: who am I? What is my purpose in life? How can I be me? What are my responsibilities to the communities I'm a part of? Why do I serve? What is leadership? How can I lead?  This course is associated with the SESP Leadership Institute. Only students enrolled in the leadership institute can register for this course.

title: Introduction to Social Science Research (1 Unit)
description: This course provides an overview of how social scientists study human behavior and society. It will introduce students to the different stages of the research process: literature review, research question formulation, research design, data collection, analysis, and presentation of findings.

title: Special Topics (1 Unit)
description: Foundational work on special topics.

title: Community Based Research Methodologies: Educational Justice (1 Unit)
description: This course examines the histories, ideas, practices, relations and possible futures that shape struggles for educational justice and human thriving. The course is unique in that it brings together an intergenerational group of thinkers and learners (high school students, undergraduate students, youth workers, graduate students, professors, high school teachers and community members) to engage in collaborative study, reflection and design.

title: Field Research Methods (1 Unit)
description: Guided practice in systematic and participant observation. Observer bias, field notes, unobtrusive measures.

title: Peer-Led Learning: Theory and Practice (0.25 Unit)
description: SESP 291 is the training program for students working as first-time mentors in the Peer Leaders program. It is taken over two academic quarters, with each quarter offering .25 credit (a total of .5 credit).

title: Peer-Led Learning: Theory and Practice (0.25 Unit)
Leadership Studio I (1 Unit)
description: Learning organizing requires building a real organization. Students accepted to the certificate program will further develop their organizing skills by taking a leadership role on the executive board of Open Democracy Northwestern (undergraduate club).  Leaders will develop strategy, train club members, manage operations.  Leaders will receive weekly coaching from instructors. Course is only open to students who have completed the first year sequence of the certificate.

title: Leadership Studio II (1 Unit)
description: Learning organizing requires building a real organization. Students accepted to the certificate program will further develop their organizing skills by taking a leadership role on the executive board of Open Democracy Northwestern (undergraduate club).  Leaders will develop strategy, train club members, manage operations.  Leaders will receive weekly coaching from instructors. Course is only open to student who have completed the first year sequence of the Civic Engagement Certificate.

title: Student Organized Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Courses proposed by students and supervised by faculty sponsors on special topics approved by the SESP undergraduate education director. May be taken only once per quarter; pass/no credit only. Consultation with the SESP student affairs assistant dean advised.

title: Causal Methods for Evaluating Policy (1 Unit)
description: This course will provide students with a framework for understanding causal inference and a toolkit for making causal claims using quantitative data.

title: Race and Education (1 Unit)
description: Conceptual underpinnings of the construct of race and how conceptions of race have influenced the course of education in the United States.

title: Crafting Child Policy (1 Unit)
description: This course is open to undergraduate students interested in the intersection of child development, social policy, and  applied  research. The course will guide students in how to apply psychological theory and rigorous research methods to the study of child and family policies in real-world settings. Working in groups, students will have the opportunity to answer pressing, current  questions posed by the Illinois  Governor’s Office of Early Childhood Education and Chicago’s Office of the Mayor. Group projects have the potential to help inform the structure and development of the state’s  innovative  policies for young children (birth to 8) and  their  families.

title: Holocaust Memory, Memorial and Museums (1 Unit)
description: What is Holocaust memory? How has Holocaust memory changed over time, and how does the Holocaust continue to affect our understanding of trauma, atrocity, and human rights today? This seminar addresses individual memory, including survivor and witness testimony, memory and trauma, and the impact of the Holocaust on survivors' families and communities.

title: Race, Adolescence, and School Discipline (1 Unit)
description: In recent years, racial disparities in school discipline have attracted the attention of educators, policymakers, parents, and the general public. Why is it so hard for legal, political, and educational institutions to improve school discipline? How do intersections of race, gender, and social class matter for students' experiences of school discipline? Are there schools that are getting discipline right? What does that look like, and to what extent can other schools learn from their successes? In this course, we will learn about evidence-based policy improvements and imagine how to create schools where race does not predict discipline.

title: Special Topics (1 Unit)
description: Advanced work on special topics.

title: Magic Monsters & the Holocaust (1 Unit)
description: In this course, we'll explore public learning about the Holocaust through popular film and fiction. We'll question which historical narratives are being told and which are being ignored, and we'll ask why and how genres like fantasy, sci-fi, fairy tales, and time travel are commonly used to bring stories of mass-violence to the public.

title: BSED/BSJ Experiential Learning Community Workshop Series (0 Unit)
description: In their second year, students choose 1 experiential education requirement – either the SESP Practicum, Student Teaching, or Medill Journalism Residency for 4 units of credit. The students participate in the Community Workshop Series during the year they complete their experiential education requirement.

title: Research Apprenticeship (1 Unit)
description: Opportunity to participate in faculty research projects.

title: Advanced Research Design (1 Unit)
description: Overview of research methods that may be used to design and implement the honors thesis.

title: Experiential Learning: Practicum (4 Units)
description: The practicum is a substantive, full quarter (four academic credits) Experiential Education requirement typically completed during a SESP undergraduate student’s junior year (inclusive of the summer before/after).

title: Senior Thesis Seminar (1-3 Units)
description: Students develop, design, implement, and evaluate a research project under a faculty advisor's guidance.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: Faculty-supervised study of special topics of the student's own choosing and not covered in regular courses.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Science in Human Culture Senior Seminar (1 Unit)
description: For students who wish to qualify for honors by writing a senior thesis.

title: Science in Human Culture Senior Seminar (1 Unit)
description: For students who wish to qualify for honors by writing a senior thesis.

title: Science in Human Culture Senior Seminar (1 Unit)
description: For students who wish to qualify for honors by writing a senior thesis.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: An independent course of study in the discipline of science studies with an affiliated faculty advisor and with the permission of that instructor.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Introduction to Russian Literature (1 Unit)
description: Comprehensive overview of the central prose works and literary movements in 19th-century Russia. 1. Thematic and formal study of major works by Pushkin, Gogol, Lermontov, Turgenev. 2. Tolstoy, Dostoevsky. 3. Turgenev, the late Tolstoy, Chekhov, Bunin.

title: Introduction to Russian Literature (1 Unit)
description: A close consideration of two great books, Dostoevsky’s Brothers Karamazov and Tolstoy’s Anna Karenina. Questions to be addressed include: What makes literature worth reading? What makes great literature great? How do realist novels work? What can we learn from these two novels about important questions in life?

title: Introduction to Russian Literature (1 Unit)
description: Comprehensive overview of the central prose works and literary movements in 19th-century Russia. 1. Thematic and formal study of major works by Pushkin, Gogol, Lermontov, Turgenev. 2. Tolstoy, Dostoevsky. 3. Turgenev, the late Tolstoy, Chekhov, Bunin.

title: 20th-Century Russian Literature (1 Unit)
description: Major works in cultural-historical context, from the revolutions of 1917 through the 1950s. Variable content depending on instructor. Focus on one of the following: Russian modernism in literature, music, film, and visual art; gender; émigré literature; socialist realism in Soviet literature and visual arts.

title: 20th-Century Russian Literature (1 Unit)
description: Major works in cultural-historical context, from the 1940s through the present. Variable content depending on instructor. Non-conformist Russian literature, film, and visual art, Russian literature and art in the transition from the Soviet to post-Soviet era. Focus on art and politics and gender representation in culture.

title: Introduction to Polish Literature (1 Unit)
description: Survey of Polish literature from the 19th century to the present. Focus on literary movements such as romanticism, realism, Young Poland, the avant-garde, Holocaust literature, and dissident and queer writings.

title: Language, Politics, & Identity (1 Unit)
description: Role of language in constructing, preserving, and manipulating political and national identities. Topics include language discrimination, linguistic nationalism, language and religion, alphabet issues, dialect issues. Regional content varies. LING 222-0 and SLAVIC 222-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Slavic Civilizations (1 Unit)
description: Culture, history, language, and literature of the Slavs historically and/or in modern periods. Content varies with instructor; may be repeated for credit.

title: Heart of Europe: Poland in the Twentieth Century (1 Unit)
description: Study of key developments in Polish history, literature, and thought by way of texts drawn from literature, history, politics, journalism, memoirs, essays, and film. Poland as a microcosm for recent European history and culture.

title: Life 101: Literature and Psychology (1 Unit)
description: A conversation among disciplines, especially psychology and literary studies. Explores how people tell stories to develop a deeper and more multifaceted understanding of life narrative and human life. SLAVIC 262-0 and HDC 262-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.

title: Czech Culture: Film, Visual Arts & Music (1 Unit)
description: Czech culture represented in film and visual arts.

title: Visual Art in the Context of Russian Culture (1 Unit)
description: Major themes related to the history of Russian art from the medieval period to the beginning of the 21st century. General survey of Russian visual art in the dual contexts of Russian culture and European visual art; topics directly related to iconography, particular artistic movements, and the philosophy of art. Variable content depending on instructor. Focus on interconnections among visual arts, literature, religion, and political history.

title: Visual Art in the Context of Russian Culture (1 Unit)
description: Word and Image in the Context of Russian Culture. Introduction to the semiotcics of both textual and visual narratives within the framework of Russian culture; study of the relation of visual representation to language and literature from medieval icons and apocrypha to contemporary artists' books and graphic novels.

title: Polish Cinema (1 Unit)
description: Development of Polish cinema from silent era to contemporary times with focus on major historical and political events in Poland in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. Special focus on what it means to study “national cinema.”.

title: Making a Dictionary: The Northwestern Project (1 Unit)
description: Creation of an online dictionary of Northwestern language. Learning about the connection between language, society, and identity; sociolinguistic fieldwork; lexicography; politics of dictionaries. LING 363-0 and SLAVIC 322-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Prague: City of Cultures, City of Conflict (1 Unit)
description: Examination of the cultural, political, and social transformation of Prague from the 19th century to the present. Cosmopolitan Prague, communist Prague, and capitalist Prague. SLAVIC 328-0 and GERMAN 328-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Russian Film (1 Unit)
description: Development of Russian film and film theory from the silent era to the 1980s. Golden Age of Russian cinema (Eisenstein, Pudovkin, Vertov, Protazanov, Vasiliev brothers, Dovzhenko, socialist realism).

title: Andrei Tarkovsky's Aesthetics and World Cinema (1 Unit)
description: Major films of Tarkovsky and of Russian and non-Russian directors whose work is related to his (Eisenstein, Wenders, Bergman, Kurosawa).

title: East European Literature and Visual Arts (1 Unit)
description: Course focuses on the intersection of literature, visual arts, film, and politics in Central and Eastern Europe. Content varies; may be repeated for credit.

title: Topics in Literature and Arts (1 Unit)
description: Content varies. May be repeated for credit.

title: Independent Study (1 Unit)
description: For majors selected as candidates for departmental honors; for other advanced students with consent of instructor.

title: College Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and introducing skills necessary to thriving at Northwestern. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: First-Year Writing Seminar (1 Unit)
description: Small, writing and discussion-oriented course exploring a specific topic or theme, and focused on the fundamentals of effective, college-level written communication. Not eligible to be applied towards a WCAS major or minor except where specifically indicated.

title: Introduction to Sociology (1 Unit)
description: Broad overview of a wide range of social issues and ways of sociological thinking. Characteristics of group life. Interrelations of society, culture, and personality; major social institutions and processes.

title: Social Inequality: Race, Class, and Power (1 Unit)
description: Origins and functions of stratification. Class, prestige, and esteem. Interaction of racial and cultural groups. Inequality in workplaces, neighborhoods, schools, families, media, and other settings.

title: Social Problems (1 Unit)
description: Emergence of social problems. How the media, politicians, lawmakers, and others define social issues. How lives and self-images are shaped when people are connected to a social problem.

title: Law and Society (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to the role of law in American society and the influence of society on law. Courts, the legal profession, law enforcement, inequality, and social change. Taught with LEGAL_ST 206-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Cities in Society (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to issues of cities and metropolitan areas, including spatial, economic, and political trends; private and public decision making; class, race, and gender; and possible solutions to inequalities and planning challenges.

title: Race and Society (1 Unit)
description: Critical analysis of the biological myth and social reality of race; factors responsible for persistent racial inequality in the United States; social and political implications of race.

title: Families and Societies (1 Unit)
description: Changes, continuities, and variations in family life in industrialized countries over the past century. Key concepts in sociology and the study of families. Explanations for changes and implications for inequality.

title: Food and Society: An Introduction (1 Unit)
description: Overview of past and present food systems from a sociological perspective, examining the roles of culture, government policy, and social movements in shaping such systems and future alternatives. ENVR_POL 211-0 and SOCIOL 211-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Environment and Society (1 Unit)
description: Key environmental problems, such as climate change and oil spills; how they are shaped by the market, government regulations, and social movements; possible solutions. SOCIOL 212-0 and ENVR_POL 212-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Economy and Society (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to sociological approaches to economic life. Topics include property rights, illegal markets, money, economic inequalities, direct sales, and boycotts.

title: Gender and Society (1 Unit)
description: How our society creates ideas of what gender and gender-appropriate behaviors are. How these ideas are linked to sexuality and relationships, and how they become part of political conflict.

title: Education and Inequality: Focus on Chicago (1 Unit)
description: Causes and consequences of educational inequality. History, educational outcomes, and recent reform efforts of Chicago Public Schools.

title: Health, Biomedicine, Culture, and Society (1 Unit)
description: Provides a broad introduction to controversies surrounding health and biomedicine by analyzing culture, politics, values, and social institutions. Taught with HUM 220-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Masculinities and Society (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to the foundational theory and research in the field of masculinities studies. How is masculinity constructed through interactions with institutions, individuals, and social structure?

title: Sociological Analysis (1 Unit)
description: Logic and methods of social research, qualitative and quantitative analysis of social data, and ethical, political, and policy issues in social research. Foundation for further work in social research.

title: Legal Studies Research Methods (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to research methodologies used in interdisciplinary legal studies, including jurisprudence and legal reasoning, qualitative and quantitative social science methods, and historical and textual analysis. Taught with SOCIOL 227-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Sexuality and Society (1 Unit)
description: Examination of the role of sexuality in the cultural, economic, political, and social organization of the United States. Sex work, sex tourism, sexual migration, LGBT social movements, and moral panics. SOCIOL 232-0 is taught with GNDR_ST 232-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Critical Thought on Race and Ethnicity (1 Unit)
description: This course examines historical and contemporary manifestations racism, ethnocentrism, nationalism, ancestry, difference, and privilege. Lectures and readings focus on practices of racialization (how individuals/groups are sorted into races), and the development of global and local racial paradigms (the rules of race-making and racial assignment), and finally centers on why these denigrating mechanisms are so difficult to eradicate.

title: Undocumented America (1 Unit)
description: How does legal status shape the lives of the 11 million Americans who are undocumented? This course develops the necessary historical context, as well as the conceptual tools to make sense of why people migrate, what shapes their incorporation, how does legal status intersect with other social differences, and how does legal status shape people’s lived experiences with social institutions. LEGAL_ST 245-0 and SOCIOL 245-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both.

title: Introductory Topics in Sociology (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to different key issues in the field. Topics vary. May be repeated for credit with a different topic.

title: Native Society: Past and Present (1 Unit)
description: Provides an overview of the culture and history of Native groups and how these histories influence modern Native America.  Explores the current economic and social experiences of Indians and tribes.

title: Institutions and Society (1 Unit)
description: Institutions in a broad societal context. How institutional frameworks apply to government, family, education, and the environment; implications of institutions. POLI_SCI 388-0 and SOCIOL 288-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: The City: Urbanization and Urbanism (1 Unit)
description: Theories of urbanization, housing, jobs, race and class, segregation, community and social networks politics, reform policies and planning. Research projects.

title: Sociology of Organizations (1 Unit)
description: Structure and function of formal organizations, especially in business and government. Stratification, social control, and conflict. Discretion, rules, and information in achieving goals. Modes of participation. Development of informal norms.

title: Analysis and Interpretation of Social Data (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to quantitative methods: the interpretation of descriptive statistics, relationships between variables, multiple regression, and the logic of inferential statistics.

title: Politics of Racial Knowledge (1 Unit)
description: Major developments in the history of racial knowledge, from Enlightenment philosophy to contemporary genomics. The intersection of politics and science in creating notions of race. Students are encouraged to take SOCIOL 208-0 prior to enrolling.

title: Population Dynamics (1 Unit)
description: Social causes and consequences of population dynamics (fertility, mortality, marriage, divorce, migration) and population structures (age, sex, size, density). Relationship between population changes and health, environmental, and economic outcomes.

title: Sociological Theory (1 Unit)
description: Sociological perspectives developed by classic theorists. Elucidation and testing of sociological principles in contemporary research. Primarily for sociology majors. Open to others with consent of instructor.

title: School and Society (1 Unit)
description: Reciprocal influences between formal institutions of education and the broader society from different theoretical perspectives. Internal organization of schools, inequality in educational settings and outcomes by gender, class, and race/ethnicity.

title: Political Sociology (1 Unit)
description: Selected topics in political economy and sociology: revolutions, the development of the modern state, third world development, international conflict, politics of memory and civil society.

title: Sociology of the Family (1 Unit)
description: Influence of socioeconomic and other structural and cultural resources and constraints on family structure and dynamics. Historical and comparative perspectives on the modern family.

title: Food, Politics and Society (1 Unit)
description: Social groups, institutions, and policies shaping food production, distribution, and consumption around the world; their social and environmental consequences. Alternatives to existing food systems. SOCIOL 311-0 and ENVR_POL 311-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Technology and Society (1 Unit)
description: Sociological approaches to understanding the dynamics of technology, culture, and inequality.

title: Sociology of Religion (1 Unit)
description: Sociological approach to the study of American religion. Communities, practices, race, gender, and politics in the shaping of religion.

title: Economic Sociology (1 Unit)
description: Sociological approach to production, distribution, consumption, and markets. Classic and contemporary approaches to the economy compared across social science disciplines.

title: Global Development (1 Unit)
description: Exploration of the economic and social changes constituting development, focusing on comparison between the historical experience in Europe and more recent processes in Africa, Asia, and Latin America. SOCIOL 317-0 and POLI_SCI 352-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Sociology of Law (1 Unit)
description: Sociological analysis of legal institutions such as courts, the police, and lawyers. Law, inequality, and social change. SOCIOL 318-0 is taught with LEGAL_ST 308-0 ; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Sociology of Science (1 Unit)
description: Science as social system. Personality, class, and cultural factors in scientific development, creativity, choice of role, simultaneous invention, and priority disputes. Social effects on objectivity and bias.

title: Gender, Health, and Medicine (1 Unit)
description: Examination of the way gender organizes health and medicine, as well as how the medical system and health practices create and organize gender.

title: Numbers, Identity & Modernity: How Calculation Shapes Who We Are & What We Know (1 Unit)
description: Investigation of how we make and use numbers, how we know ourselves through numbers, the kinds of authority we grant to numbers, and how numbers inform our ethics.

title: Sociology of Immigration (1 Unit)
description: Sociological approach to immigration addressing such issues as assimilation, race/ethnicity, gender, transnationalism.

title: American Subcultures and Ethnic Groups (1 Unit)
description: Differentiation, organization, and stratification by ethnicity, race, lifestyle, and other traits. Maintenance of subgroup boundaries and distinctiveness. Consequences of difference: identity, political and economic participation, group solidarity.

title: Global Capitalism (1 Unit)
description: Sociological aspects of the rise of industrial capitalism. Rise of industrial capitalism in Europe, different forms of capitalism across the world, and consequences for poverty and inequality. Development and underdevelopment.

title: Global & Local Inequalities (1 Unit)
description: Inequalities in economic and social status, including in income, health, politics, social policy, the family, gender, and race. Contemporary US focus but also historical and global trends.

title: Youth and Society (1 Unit)
description: How modern definitions of childhood and adolescence have evolved. Diversity across the lives of young people today and the development of social networks and transitions to adulthood.

title: Inequality & American Society (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to research on social stratification and inequality, focusing on American society. Theories of distributive justice, trends, intergenerational mobility, gender and race inequality, causes and consequences of inequalities.

title: Field Research and Methods of Data Collection (1 Unit)
description: Practicum in firsthand data collection using observation and structured and unstructured interviewing. Issues of reliability and validity and qualitative analysis.

title: Law, Markets, and Globalization (1 Unit)
description: The role of national and international law in recent economic globalization trends, global convergence in law, legal transplants, globalization and the environment.

title: Markets, Hierarchies & Democracies (1 Unit)
description: The forms and social structures for making economic and political decisions in modern societies.

title: Capitalism, Labor, and Gender (1 Unit)
description: Sociological perspectives on labor in the context of capitalism. Labor, gender, class, race, status, and power. Production, reproduction, technology, and occupational structure; organization of workplace.

title: Social Protest and Social Change Around the World (1 Unit)
description: How and why social protests can initiate major social change within societies and social groups around the world.

title: Sociology of Rational Decision Making (1 Unit)
description: Analysis of the role played by numerical and quantitative information in organizational decision making in the private and public sectors.

title: The Climate Crisis, Policies, and Society (1 Unit)
description: Examination of main impacts of climate change and of different perspectives toward mitigation and adaptation: market-based, institutionalist, bio-environmentalist, social movement, and climate justice. SOCIOL 336-0 and ENVR_POL 336-0 are taught together; may not receive credit for both courses.

title: Social Networks (1 Unit)
description: Introduction to the basic concepts of network analysis. Insights about social networks impacts on families, careers, health, politics, and economic life.
